title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
This report provides practical value for money (VfM) evaluation framework guidance for evaluators in the defence sector. It is not official policy. It is grounded in Oxford Policy Management’s rubric-based approach to VfM assessment (King et al. 2023) and Verian’s Value for Investment guidance (King and Hurrell 2024), then adapts those ideas to defence capability, digital and cyber, infrastructure, and personnel programmes.
This report explains how to apply a defence VfM evaluation. The Defence VfI Rubric Generation report sits alongside it and explains how to build the criteria, subcriteria, standards, and evidence plan before applying the evaluation. Use that report when the framework itself needs to be generated or checked; use this report when the framework is being applied to an evaluation question.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Evaluation Workflow
Verian’s Value for Investment process has two phases: design and implementation. The design phase covers understanding the programme, defining the value proposition and evaluation questions, setting criteria, setting standards, and determining the evidence needed. The implementation phase covers gathering evidence, analysing evidence, synthesising judgements through the criteria and standards, and reporting clear answers (King and Hurrell 2024).

Using Source Guidance Within the Five Es
This report keeps the five Es as the organising criteria: economy, efficiency, effectiveness, equity, and cost-effectiveness. Other important themes, including risk, resilience, learning, capability, evidence quality, commercial assurance, and efficiency savings, should normally be handled as subcriteria, standards, or evidence tests within those five criteria rather than added as separate criteria.
Use the sources in different ways:







Source
Role within the five Es




OPM and Verian
Provide the judgement architecture: value proposition, criteria, subcriteria, standards, evidence, synthesis, and transparent trade-offs (King et al. 2023; King and Hurrell 2024).


Green Book
Anchors objectives, public value, options, monetised and unmonetised effects, distribution, risk, uncertainty, and decision advice (HM Treasury 2026, 2022).


JSP 507
Supplies defence appraisal and evaluation evidence on requirements, whole-life cost, CEA, COEIA, affordability, risk, optimism bias, and outturn evaluation (Ministry of Defence 2014a, 2014b).


Sourcing guidance
Strengthens economy, equity, and cost-effectiveness through delivery model assessment, market health, should-cost evidence, bid evaluation, supplier resilience, risk allocation, pricing, KPIs, and benefits evidence (Cabinet Office and Government Commercial Function 2026).


Government Efficiency Framework
Strengthens efficiency evidence by testing baselines, counterfactuals, net savings, recurrence, double counting, assurance, and protection of outputs and outcomes (HM Treasury 2025a, 2025b).





1. Understand the Programme
Start with the programme, not with indicators. Establish the decision context, users, stakeholders, information needs, programme boundaries, lifecycle stage, and constraints. In defence, this should include the operational problem, acquisition route, industrial context, security and safety constraints, support model, dependencies with other programmes, and the decision the evaluation must inform.
Develop or review the theory of change at this stage. The theory of change should describe how defence resources and inputs are expected to support activities, outputs, outcomes, and wider impacts. For a defence programme, it should make the causal pathway explicit:

resources and inputs: funding, people, contracts, facilities, data, equipment, intellectual property, permissions, and relationships;
activities: design, procurement, build, integration, testing, training, deployment, support, adaptation, and assurance;
outputs: delivered equipment, software, services, facilities, trained users, doctrine, support arrangements, and data products;
outcomes: operational availability, decision advantage, deterrence, resilience, welfare, safety, interoperability, productivity, or estate quality;
impacts: national security effect, readiness, alliance contribution, sovereign choice, public value, industrial capacity, environmental performance, and future-force optionality.



2. Define the Value Proposition and Evaluation Questions
Extend the theory of change with a value proposition. Verian describes this as making value creation explicit: who the investment is valuable to, what resources are invested, how the investment converts resources into value, and what ways of working enable that value (King and Hurrell 2024).
For a defence programme, write a short value proposition covering:

who values the investment: front-line users, commands, maintainers, allies, suppliers, communities, taxpayers, and future programmes;
what value is expected: operational effect, resilience, deterrence, interoperability, safety, personnel welfare, industrial capacity, environmental value, sovereign options, and learning;
what resources are consumed: acquisition, support, workforce, facilities, data, training, assurance, opportunity cost, and through-life funding;
what alternatives exist: doing less, delaying, upgrading existing capability, buying off the shelf, partnering with allies, leasing, using commercial services, or choosing a non-materiel option;
what must be true for value to emerge: user adoption, integration, security, supportability, realistic training, supplier performance, governance, and credible threat or demand assumptions.

Turn this into a small number of evaluation questions. The main VfM question should ask whether the resource use is justified in context; subquestions should map to the criteria, standards, and evidence plan.
This follows OPM’s principle that VfM criteria must be specific to the programme rather than copied from generic definitions (King et al. 2023). It also reflects Verian’s argument that VfM assessment should integrate economic, social, environmental, cultural, and other public values rather than treating cost-benefit analysis as the whole answer (King and Hurrell 2024).


3. Set Criteria for Good Resource Use
Use the 5Es as the criteria structure, then translate each criterion into defence language. Do not assume each E has equal weight in every programme. Do not create extra top-level criteria unless a separate governance process requires it. In this report, risk, resilience, learning, capability, evidence quality, sourcing, and assurance are treated as subcriteria, standards, or evidence checks within the five Es.








Criterion
Defence interpretation
Evaluator prompt




Economy
Stewardship of inputs, contracts, workforce, facilities, support, data, and scarce specialist capacity.
Were the right inputs secured at appropriate quality and cost, with credible commercial discipline and risk control?


Efficiency
Conversion of resources into outputs through delivery, governance, integration, testing, learning, and relationships.
Did the programme avoid avoidable delay, rework, duplication, idle capacity, and transaction cost?


Effectiveness
Operational, organisational, and user value created by the delivered capability.
Does the capability work in the conditions that matter, with users able to exploit it?


Equity
Fair distribution of benefits, burdens, access, risks, and future liabilities.
Are personnel, commands, allies, suppliers, communities, taxpayers, and future budgets treated fairly?


Cost-effectiveness
Whether the total value created justifies the total resource use compared with credible alternatives.
Is the defence effect worth the acquisition, support, risk, opportunity, and through-life cost?



After selecting criteria, define a small number of subcriteria for each one before selecting indicators or evidence. In the OPM approach, the sequence matters: understand the value proposition, select relevant criteria, write programme-specific definitions, identify subcriteria, define standards, and only then decide what evidence is needed (King et al. 2023). Verian’s Value for Investment guidance follows the same logic by using criteria and standards to make wider public value, trade-offs, and non-monetised effects explicit (King and Hurrell 2024).
Subcriteria should describe the dimensions of performance that would make resource use good in the programme context. They are not a generic checklist and they are not simply data headings. They should be derived from the value proposition, theory of change, decision context, stakeholder perspectives, and the main trade-offs the evaluation needs to judge.
Good subcriteria usually do four things:

make each criterion specific enough to judge, for example by distinguishing input stewardship, output quality, access, risks, contribution, trade-offs, or non-monetised value;
cover the most material parts of the resource-to-value pathway without trying to measure everything;
separate different kinds of performance that might otherwise be hidden inside one rating, such as delivery speed, output quality, targeting, learning, and coordination;
support standards and evidence planning, so evaluators can later say what would count as poor, adequate, good, or excellent performance.

Subcriteria should generally follow the main question each E is asking rather than repeat programme-specific evidence lists. Efficiency needs particular care: a narrow cost-per-output view may be useful for some activities, but it can miss important features of complex capability, digital, infrastructure, and personnel programmes. Following OPM’s approach, efficiency subcriteria should distinguish technical, allocative, dynamic, and relational efficiency where those distinctions matter.








Criterion
Subcriteria should clarify
Generic subcriteria focus




Economy
Which inputs need stewardship, what quality threshold is required, and which cost or probity risks would undermine value.
Price reasonableness, input quality, procurement discipline, probity, cost drivers, stewardship, and risks of false economy.


Efficiency
How resources are converted into outputs, whether the right activities are prioritised, whether learning improves resource use, and whether relationships reduce waste.
Technical efficiency: unit or activity cost, timeliness, output quality, budget adherence, process productivity, and avoidable rework. Allocative efficiency: portfolio balance, targeting, prioritisation, option choices, stopping lower-value activity, and shifting resources to higher-value opportunities. Dynamic efficiency: feedback loops, experimentation, learning from failure, incremental improvement, timely adaptation, and productivity over time. Relational efficiency: trust, coordination, partner alignment, information sharing, dispute resolution, reduced duplication, and lower transaction costs.


Effectiveness
Which outputs must work, for whom, under what conditions, and how they plausibly contribute to intended outcomes.
Output quality, outcome contribution, causal assumptions, user or beneficiary uptake, unintended effects, and contextual conditions.


Equity
How benefits, burdens, access, risks, and future liabilities are distributed across stakeholders and over time.
Inclusion, access, differential outcomes, distribution of costs and risks, burden sharing, dignity, and intergenerational effects.


Cost-effectiveness
Which outcomes or impacts justify the resources used, what alternatives are credible, and how monetised and non-monetised value will be weighed.
Costs relative to outcomes, alternatives, opportunity cost, uncertainty, sustainability, equity goals, and qualitative value judgement.



These efficiency types should be used selectively. For example, technical efficiency may dominate a mature equipment delivery question, while dynamic and relational efficiency may be more important for cyber, digital, innovation, partnering, or complex transformation programmes.
If an efficiency or savings claim is material, test it against Government Efficiency Framework-style evidence. A credible efficiency claim should distinguish forecast from realised savings, state the baseline and counterfactual, net off costs to deliver, show whether savings are recurring, prevent double counting, and demonstrate that outputs, outcomes, quality, safety, security, or strategic priorities have not deteriorated. Underspend, cancellation, delayed spending, inflation changes, reduced output, unfunded transfer of work, cost shifting to another organisation, or unsupported forecast savings should not be treated as efficiency evidence without a separate value argument.
The detailed defence subcriteria and evidence prompts later in this report show how these general design prompts can be translated into programme-specific assessment headings.
The 5Es are often most useful for ex-post or in-flight assessment. For ex-ante decisions, coordinate with the Green Book five-case model and use explicit criteria to make the strategic, economic, commercial, financial, and management cases easier to judge coherently.


4. Set Standards Before Interpreting Evidence
OPM’s practical contribution is the disciplined link from criteria to standards, evidence, and judgement (King et al. 2023). For each criterion, agree performance standards before judging the evidence.







Level
Defence VfM standard




Excellent
Performance exceeds reasonable expectations; trade-offs are explicit; evidence shows strong delivery of operational value, stewardship, learning, and fair distribution of burdens.


Good
Performance meets expectations with minor exceptions; material risks are controlled; evidence supports a credible positive judgement.


Adequate
Minimum expectations are met, but important weaknesses remain in cost, schedule, usability, integration, support, evidence, or distribution of burdens.


Poor
Minimum expectations are not met, or unresolved weaknesses make the use of resources hard to justify.



Rubrics should be specific enough to guide judgement. For example, “good effectiveness” for a satellite communications programme is not the same as “good effectiveness” for accommodation modernisation. The former may turn on assured communications, contested-environment resilience, and allied interoperability; the latter may turn on living conditions, occupancy, maintainability, and effects on personnel welfare.
For each E, standards should also state what evidence confidence is required. A “good” rating should not rest on weak source traceability, untested assumptions, unsupported efficiency claims, or evidence that covers only monetised effects while omitting material operational, social, environmental, distributional, or future value. Where evidence is partial, commercially sensitive, classified, or immature, the rating should explain how that limitation affects confidence.


5. Identify the Evidence and Methods Needed
Use economic analysis where it is feasible, but do not let it crowd out other evidence. Verian’s Value for Investment approach is useful for defence because many important effects are uncertain, classified, strategic, or difficult to monetise (King and Hurrell 2024).
Common evidence sources include:

business cases, approvals, commercial strategies, contract data, cost models, and cost assurance;
schedules, risk registers, issue logs, test results, acceptance evidence, and support data;
operational analysis, trials, exercises, user research, training records, and readiness data;
cyber, safety, security, resilience, interoperability, and data-governance evidence;
supplier, workforce, estate, environmental, community, and industrial-base evidence;
delivery model assessments, market health evidence, should-cost models, bid evaluation records, KPI baselines, payment mechanisms, risk allocation matrices, supplier financial standing checks, continuity plans, and benefits reports;
GEF-style efficiency evidence, including baselines, counterfactuals, gross and net savings, costs to deliver, recurrence, double-counting controls, outcome protection, and assurance records;
comparator evidence from allies, commercial alternatives, previous programmes, or portfolio options;
stakeholder evidence from users, front-line commands, maintainers, digital operators, commercial staff, suppliers, local communities, and allied partners.

At this point, decide which economic evidence is proportionate. JSP 507 is clear that full cost-benefit analysis has a limited role in MOD because few defence activities produce benefits that can sensibly be valued in monetary terms; most MOD appraisals therefore use cost-effectiveness analysis, and many equipment decisions use Combined Operational Effectiveness and Investment Appraisal to compare through-life cost with operational or business effectiveness (Ministry of Defence 2014a, 2014b). Treat CBA as exceptional rather than the default. Use it only where the material benefits can credibly be monetised, such as some health, safety, environmental, business-change, or spend-to-save cases, and seek Defence Economics advice where JSP 507 requires it. For most defence VfM evaluations, prioritise whole-life cost, cost-effectiveness or COEIA-style option comparison, break-even analysis, scenario and sensitivity analysis, and rubric-based judgement of non-monetised operational, strategic, resilience, safety, welfare, and future value. Where any monetised analysis is used, state clearly what it covers, what it omits, and how the omitted value affects the overall VfM judgement. The JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation source note maps the guidance to VfI-style criteria, standards, and evidence prompts.


Evidence Quality and Assurance
Evidence quality is not a separate top-level criterion in this report. It is a discipline that applies across all five Es. For each major evidence item, record what it informs, how it has been checked, what it cannot show, and how much weight it should carry in synthesis.










Evidence item
Five-E use
Quality checks
Common limitation
Use in synthesis




Business case and options appraisal
Economy, effectiveness, cost-effectiveness.
Check objectives, option set, critical success factors, appraisal boundary, treatment of unmonetised value, sensitivity, and optimism bias.
Forecast evidence may not match delivery reality.
Establish expected value, alternatives, and assumptions to test.


Whole-life cost and should-cost models
Economy and cost-effectiveness.
Check cost completeness, base date, inflation, hidden costs, common costs, transfer payments, risk, assurance, and treatment of retained-client costs.
Future operating, upgrade, or dependency costs may be immature.
Judge cost realism, affordability, and false economy risk.


Efficiency and benefits records
Efficiency and cost-effectiveness.
Check baseline, counterfactual, gross and net treatment, costs to deliver, recurrence, double counting, and no adverse outcome impact.
Savings may be forecast, deferred, shifted, or dependent on unproven service changes.
Distinguish claimed, forecast, delivered, and assured efficiency.


Commercial and supplier evidence
Economy, equity, and cost-effectiveness.
Check delivery model rationale, market evidence, competition, framework route, bid evaluation, risk allocation, pricing, supplier financial standing, continuity, and benefits ownership.
Supplier data may be commercially sensitive or incomplete.
Judge whether sourcing choices support sustainable value.


Operational, user, and service evidence
Effectiveness, equity, and cost-effectiveness.
Check relevance to operational conditions, user groups, readiness, quality, safety, cyber, security, and maintainability.
Trials or service data may not represent deployed conditions or all users.
Judge whether outputs plausibly create intended defence value.


Stakeholder and qualitative evidence
Effectiveness and equity.
Check sampling, perspective coverage, triangulation, dissenting views, and classified or sensitive exclusions.
Evidence may be partial or shaped by role, incentives, or access constraints.
Explain how and why value is created or blocked.





Rubric Readiness Check
Before gathering evidence, check that the five-E framework is usable:

each criterion has a programme-specific definition linked to the value proposition;
subcriteria are limited to the material dimensions of value and resource use;
standards describe poor, adequate, good, and excellent performance before evidence is interpreted;
sourcing, risk, resilience, learning, assurance, and evidence quality are located inside the five Es rather than added as extra criteria;
efficiency or savings claims have planned evidence for baselines, counterfactuals, netting, recurrence, double counting, and outcome protection;
commercial value evidence covers delivery model, market health, should-cost, supplier resilience, risk allocation, pricing, KPIs, and benefits realisation where material;
monetised and non-monetised value are both visible;
alternatives, opportunity cost, affordability, uncertainty, distribution, and future liabilities are visible;
known evidence gaps and confidence implications are recorded.



6. Gather Evidence
Collect the agreed evidence in a way that follows the rubric. Survey, interview, document-review, trial-analysis, and administrative-data tools should use headings aligned to the criteria and subcriteria so evidence can be organised efficiently.
Defence evaluations should plan for practical constraints: classified evidence, operational security, small user populations, immature outcome data, long benefit horizons, supplier confidentiality, and contested causal claims. These constraints should be recorded as evidence limitations, not hidden in the final judgement.


7. Analyse Evidence
Analyse each evidence stream separately before synthesis. Cost data, schedule data, test evidence, operational analysis, user feedback, cyber assurance, estate data, and supplier evidence each need their own quality checks.
This is also where causal inference or contribution analysis should be handled. For some defence questions, a counterfactual may be available through options analysis, historical comparison, allied benchmarking, or status quo modelling. For others, a theory-based contribution claim will be more credible than forced monetisation.


8. Synthesize Judgements and Report
The final VfM judgement should show the reasoning chain:

programme context and theory of change;
value proposition and evaluation questions;
tailored criteria and subcriteria;
agreed standards;
evidence by criterion;
analysis, ratings, and uncertainty;
trade-offs between criteria;
overall judgement;
actions to improve future resource use.

Avoid averaging scores mechanically. Defence programmes often involve explicit trade-offs: higher acquisition cost may be justified by survivability, safety, interoperability, resilience, or lower through-life cost. The evaluator’s task is to explain whether those trade-offs are justified by the evidence.
Report the answer up front. The report should give clear evidence-supported ratings against the criteria, then show the reasoning and limitations behind those ratings.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Defence Criteria and Evidence Prompts

Economy: Resource Stewardship
Focus on whether the programme secured appropriate inputs at appropriate cost and quality.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Commercial discipline
Procurement strategy, competition records, sole-source justification, open-book evidence, price benchmarking, should-cost analysis.


Market and delivery model choice
Delivery model assessment, market health analysis, framework route rationale, insourcing or outsourcing comparison, early market engagement records.


Whole-life cost control
Acquisition, support, infrastructure, training, workforce, digital, disposal, decommissioning, and upgrade cost models.


Scarce capability inputs
Specialist engineering, nuclear, cyber, software, shipbuilding, aviation, construction, commercial, and safety skills.


Long-lead and obsolescence management
Long-lead item plans, spares, technology roadmaps, refresh cycles, design-change control, inventory strategy.


Supplier financial standing and continuity
Economic and financial standing checks, continuity plans, resolution plans, dependency mapping, critical supplier monitoring.


Security and quality investment
Assurance, test, certification, safety, cyber, data protection, and quality-at-source decisions that prevent later rework or failure.





Efficiency: Delivery and Learning
Focus on whether resources were converted into useful outputs without avoidable waste.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Schedule realism
Integrated master schedule, risk adjustment, critical path, dependency tracking, approvals history.


Integration and test flow
Systems integration evidence, trials plans, certification progress, defect and rework logs, acceptance evidence.


Governance speed
Decision logs, escalation routes, approvals time, change-control performance, delegated authority.


Supply-chain and resource flow
Supplier performance, material availability, yard or facility capacity, workforce allocation, competing programme demands.


Valid efficiency and savings claims
Baselines, counterfactuals, gross and net savings, costs to deliver, outturns, recurrence evidence, double-counting controls, outcome and quality protection.


Dynamic and relational efficiency
Learning logs, adaptation decisions, joint working arrangements, information sharing, dispute resolution, lessons applied across projects.





Effectiveness: Operational Value
Focus on whether the delivered outputs plausibly create intended defence outcomes.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Capability delivered
Acceptance trials, operational test and evaluation, performance against key parameters, mission-thread evidence.


Reliability, availability, maintainability
Availability data, defect rates, mean time between failures, support burden, spares availability, maintainer feedback.


Interoperability
Allied, joint, and legacy-system integration evidence; standards compliance; exercise results; data-sharing evidence.


User adoption and readiness
Training completion, user feedback, doctrine and tactics updates, simulator use, operational readiness indicators.


Service quality and benefits realisation
KPI baselines, benefits register, service management data, incident resolution, user satisfaction, quality thresholds, benefits owner evidence.


Resilience and adaptability
Cyber resilience, contested-environment performance, safety, upgrade path, modularity, technical debt, climate resilience where relevant.





Equity: Distribution of Benefits and Burdens
Focus on fairness across stakeholders and time.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Risk allocation
Contract terms, liability, incentives, safety responsibilities, cyber responsibilities, insurance, decommissioning obligations.


User and maintainer burden
Training load, supportability, operating cost, workload, safety, welfare, usability, deployment constraints.


Cross-force and allied access
Allocation across services, units, geographies, readiness priorities, allied interoperability, coalition use.


Supplier access and market fairness
SME participation, framework access, competition design, barriers to entry, social value evidence, treatment of incumbents and new entrants.


Industrial and workforce distribution
Regional effects, skills pipelines, sovereign capability, workshare, supplier resilience, workforce transition, knowledge transfer.


Inter-generational fairness
Technical debt, deferred maintenance, disposal liabilities, carbon, estate condition, future budget pressure.





Cost-Effectiveness: Value Relative to Alternatives
Focus on whether the outcomes justify the resources used.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Total cost of ownership
Acquisition, operation, sustainment, infrastructure, training, refresh, disposal, and opportunity cost.


Alternative options
Delivery model assessment; commercial service, allied collaboration, upgrade, lease, framework route, off-the-shelf buy, smaller scope, slower delivery, or non-materiel solution.


Strategic and operational value
Deterrence, resilience, readiness, interoperability, mission success, personnel welfare, information advantage.


Wider public value
Industrial capacity, exports, skills, environmental gains, community effects, cross-government reuse.


Commercial value and incentives
Bid evaluation evidence, price-quality trade-offs, KPI design, payment mechanism, risk pricing, benefits realisation, contract management evidence.


Uncertainty and sensitivity
Scenario analysis, optimism bias, cost risk, threat changes, demand changes, technology cycles.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
section: Applying the Guidance by Programme Type
text:
Applying the Guidance by Programme Type

Programme Contexts and VfM Emphasis
Use the programme descriptions as adaptation prompts rather than generic case studies. The point is to tailor the VfM framework to the main value mechanism, delivery constraints, users, and alternatives for the programme being assessed.







Programme type
VfM emphasis




Combat air
Integration of sensors, weapons, software, support, training, international partners, mission data, and through-life upgrade cost.


Submarines
Nuclear or specialist safety, stealth, industrial capacity, long-lead items, assurance, decommissioning liabilities, and strategic deterrence value.


Surface warships
Shipyard flow, combat-system integration, fleet availability, interoperability, crew burden, growth margins, and upgrade path.


Armoured vehicles
Protection, mobility, firepower, weight trade-offs, maintainability, soldier usability, fleet commonality, and alternative land-effect options.


Digital and cyber programmes
Data, connectivity, software quality, cyber and anti-jamming resilience, user adoption, service reliability, security accreditation, interoperability, technology refresh, open standards, and avoidance of supplier lock-in.


Infrastructure and estate programmes
Whole-life asset cost, planning and environmental constraints, construction and transition risk, maintainability, occupancy, user experience, community effects, land-disposal value, carbon, biodiversity, and future estate liabilities.


Major personnel programmes
Workforce demand forecasting, attraction, selection, medical and security vetting, onboarding, training pipeline capacity, candidate experience, diversity and fairness, retention into trained strength, contractor performance, and cost per successful recruit.



For each programme, define what “good” means against the intended defence outcome and programme-specific value proposition, not against a generic list of desirable features.
Major capability programmes usually create value by integrating equipment, people, information, support, doctrine, and industrial capacity into an operational effect. Crowsnest illustrates the point because it was intended to provide airborne surveillance, control, and early warning for Carrier Strike by fitting radar and mission-system equipment to Merlin helicopters (National Audit Office 2020). A VfM assessment should therefore look beyond whether a sensor was procured. It should test whether the role-fit works with the aircraft, crews, ships, command-and-control arrangements, training pipeline, support model, and deployment schedule; whether delays or integration compromises created a capability gap or reduced operational availability; and whether the chosen approach remained preferable to alternatives once through-life cost, upgrade path, and dependencies with the wider carrier strike system are considered.
Digital programmes create value through data, connectivity, software, resilience, and user adoption. They need VfM criteria that recognise rapid technology cycles and cyber risk.
Skynet 6 illustrates the point. MOD describes it as the next-generation military satellite communications programme, with more than £5 billion being invested in the Skynet programme over ten years and objectives including resilient global services, space workforce development, prosperity, contested-space capability, and net zero (Ministry of Defence 2025). A VfM assessment should therefore examine commercial arrangements, satellite, ground and user segments, cyber and anti-jamming resilience, allied interoperability, workforce capacity, alternatives such as commercial SATCOM, and through-life replacement cycles.
Do not treat system uptime, agile velocity, or licence cost as sufficient VfM evidence by themselves. Digital VfM depends on whether users can exploit the system securely and reliably in operational conditions.
A digital support contract shows how sourcing and GEF prompts can be absorbed into the existing five Es without creating new criteria. Economy would test the delivery model, framework route, should-cost model, retained-client cost, supplier standing, and false economy risks. Efficiency would test whether claimed savings are supported by a credible baseline, counterfactual, costs to deliver, recurrence, double-counting controls, and protected service quality. Effectiveness would test incident resolution, cyber compliance, service availability, user experience, and benefits realisation. Equity would test whether risk, workload, access, and service burdens are shifted unfairly to users, maintainers, smaller suppliers, or future budgets. Cost-effectiveness would test whether the whole-life cost is justified by better support, reduced duplication, cyber assurance, and credible alternatives.
Infrastructure programmes create value through places where personnel live, train, work, and operate. They need VfM criteria that account for long asset lives, planning and environmental constraints, communities, maintainability, and user experience.
The Defence Estate Optimisation portfolio is the main public example. MOD describes it as Defence’s largest estates change programme, investing £5.1 billion in modern, greener, and sustainable infrastructure, delivering accommodation for over 27,000 personnel and families, releasing surplus land for more than 32,000 homes, and forecasting over 200 hectares of biodiversity net-gain measures (Ministry of Defence 2026). Evaluation should therefore combine cost and schedule evidence with estate rationalisation, personnel outcomes, biodiversity, land-disposal value, community effects, and future estate liabilities. The NAO’s report on optimising the defence estate provides an important accountability source for judging delivery risks and assumptions (National Audit Office 2021).
Major personnel programmes create value by sustaining the workforce needed for defence outputs. The Armed Forces Recruiting Programme is a useful example because the evaluation plan frames it as a move from three separate recruitment processes to a tri-service recruitment approach, supported by recruitment, transition, digital, data, estate, and cross-government inputs (Ministry of Defence 2024). A VfM assessment should therefore test whether recruitment resources increase the number, quality, suitability, and timeliness of recruits who progress into initial training and trained strength, while reducing candidate leakage, duplicated effort, avoidable estate cost, data inconsistency, and unfair barriers. It should not treat applications, marketing reach, or starts as sufficient evidence by themselves. The judgement needs to distinguish intermediate recruitment activity from the personnel outcomes that matter for readiness, resilience, diversity, and scarce skills.
Programme types often overlap. A recruitment programme may depend on digital services, medical assessment capacity, estate choices, and training pipeline constraints. A cyber programme may include infrastructure, supplier, workforce, and operational-change elements. Evaluators should therefore tailor the criteria to the value mechanism and decision context rather than relying on the programme label.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Reporting Template
Use a consistent reporting structure so readers can audit the judgement:

Decision context: What decision will the VfM assessment support?
Theory of change: How are resources expected to create defence outputs, outcomes, and wider impacts?
Value proposition: What value was expected, for whom, through what mechanisms, and compared with what alternatives?
Evaluation questions: What answers do decision-makers and stakeholders need?
Tailored criteria: How were the 5Es translated into programme-specific definitions?
Standards: What would poor, adequate, good, and excellent performance look like?
Evidence and methods: What evidence was used, what quality checks were applied, what was missing, and how credible is it?
Analysis and judgements: What rating is given for each criterion, and why?
Trade-offs and uncertainty: Which weaknesses are tolerable, which are material, and which undermine VfM?
Overall conclusion: Is resource use justified in context?
Improvement actions: What should change in delivery, future approvals, commercial strategy, data, evaluation, or portfolio decisions?

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Common Risks

Retrofitting standards after seeing the evidence.
Treating cost control as the whole of VfM.
Ignoring users, maintainers, cyber operators, safety specialists, or local communities.
Counting industrial or strategic benefits without evidence that they are additional.
Reporting a single overall rating without explaining trade-offs and uncertainty.
Using generic 5E definitions that do not reflect the actual defence capability.
Confusing delivery outputs with sustained operational or welfare outcomes.
Treating underspend, deferral, cancellation, cost shifting, or unsupported forecast savings as efficiency.
Treating procurement compliance, bid scores, or contract award as enough evidence of commercial value.
Ignoring evidence limitations where data are classified, commercially sensitive, immature, or not representative of operational conditions.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Conclusion
The strongest defence VfM evaluations combine OPM’s explicit criteria, standards, evidence, and judgement discipline with Verian’s broader Value for Investment framing. That combination is well suited to defence because the value of a programme often lies in resilience, deterrence, interoperability, safety, welfare, sovereign choice, and future optionality as well as monetised benefits.
Evaluators should therefore design VfM assessment as a transparent reasoning process. The question is not simply whether the programme spent less, delivered more, or produced a favourable ratio. It is whether the total resource use was justified by the operational, organisational, strategic, social, environmental, and future value created.
For detailed guidance on generating the rubric architecture before applying it, see the Defence VfI Rubric Generation report.

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
This note uses Julian King’s 2019 blog post on value for money criteria as the primary source (King 2019). It reads that post alongside the OECD DAC evaluation criteria and existing notes on Value for Investment, OPM King VfM Guidance, and OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria.
The main source pages are:

Julian King & Associates: Value for Money criteria
OECD: Evaluation Criteria
OECD: Better Criteria for Better Evaluation
OECD: Applying Evaluation Criteria Thoughtfully

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
This note uses Julian King’s 2019 blog post on value for money criteria as the primary source (King 2019). It reads that post alongside the OECD DAC evaluation criteria and existing notes on Value for Investment, OPM King VfM Guidance, and OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria.
The main source pages are:

Julian King & Associates: Value for Money criteria
OECD: Evaluation Criteria
OECD: Better Criteria for Better Evaluation
OECD: Applying Evaluation Criteria Thoughtfully

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
Core Argument
Value for Investment and the OECD DAC criteria are complementary. The DAC criteria provide broad evaluative lenses for judging an intervention’s merit or worth. VfI provides a practical reasoning system for judging whether the resources invested are justified by the value created.
Put simply: the DAC criteria help structure what should be examined; VfI helps make and explain the judgement about whether the intervention was a good use of resources.
This means VfI should not be treated as a replacement for the DAC criteria. It can use DAC criteria as part of the value framework, then add explicit standards, evidence requirements, synthesis, and judgement about trade-offs.

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
King’s VfI Framing
King frames value for money as an evaluative question about how well resources are used and whether that resource use is justified (King 2019). That question needs evaluative reasoning: a transparent and logical way of moving from evidence to judgement.
In King’s account, criteria and standards do practical work. Criteria define the aspects of value for money that matter. Standards define the levels of performance needed for the resource use to count as poor, adequate, good, or excellent. Together, they help identify evidence needs, organise analysis, interpret results, make robust judgements, and tell a clear performance story.
King also argues that criteria should be determined by values and context. The familiar Five Es used in development programmes - economy, efficiency, effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, and equity - are a useful starting point, but not the final word. Other criteria may matter, including sustainability, ethical resource use, cultural fit, risk, affordability, relational efficiency, dignity, fairness, and learning.

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
OECD DAC Framing
The OECD DAC criteria are relevance, coherence, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability (OECD 2019, 2021). The OECD describes them as a normative framework for judging the merit or worth of an intervention.
The criteria are not a methodology. They define questions to be answered about an intervention and should be applied thoughtfully, in light of the evaluation purpose, stakeholders, intervention, and context. The six criteria work as complementary lenses:

Relevance asks whether the intervention is doing the right things.
Coherence asks how well the intervention fits with other policies, interventions, actors, and systems.
Effectiveness asks whether the intervention is achieving its objectives.
Efficiency asks how well resources are being used.
Impact asks what difference the intervention makes.
Sustainability asks whether benefits are likely to last.

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
Where They Overlap
The clearest overlap is efficiency, because both VfI and DAC evaluation are concerned with resource use. But VfI is broader than efficiency. King’s framing asks three linked questions: what was invested, what resulted, and whether it was worth it.
Those three questions cut across the DAC criteria:








VfI question
Related DAC criteria
How the relationship works




What did we put in?
Relevance, coherence, efficiency
The evaluation examines whether resources, design, partnerships, timing, and delivery arrangements were appropriate for the need and context.


What did we get out?
Effectiveness, impact, sustainability
The evaluation examines outputs, outcomes, wider effects, durability of benefits, and differential results across groups.


Was it worth it?
All six criteria
The evaluation synthesises evidence about need, fit, results, resource use, wider effects, and durability into a justified value judgement.



This is why DAC criteria can support VfI, but cannot by themselves complete a VfI assessment. They organise inquiry, but the evaluator still needs to define standards and explain how mixed evidence is weighed.

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
How VfI Extends DAC-Based Evaluation
VfI adds four disciplines that are often implicit in DAC-based evaluations.
First, VfI makes the overall resource-use question explicit. A DAC evaluation can answer separate questions about relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability without clearly stating whether the investment was justified overall. VfI requires the overall judgement to be made and explained.
Second, VfI requires standards. DAC criteria name dimensions of merit, but they do not automatically say what level of performance is good enough. VfI asks what counts as adequate, good, or excellent value for the scale, risk, ambition, and opportunity cost of the investment.
Third, VfI foregrounds trade-offs. An intervention may be highly relevant but costly, efficient but inequitable, effective but unsustainable, or coherent with current policy but weak on long-term impact. VfI requires the evaluation to explain how those tensions affect the final judgement.
Fourth, VfI allows context-specific criteria. DAC criteria are broad and widely recognised, but King’s post is clear that criteria should reflect values and context. A VfI framework can therefore use DAC criteria while adding criteria such as equity, cultural value, ethical resource use, resilience, capability, or learning where they are material to the value proposition.

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
Practical Synthesis
A practical way to combine the two frameworks is to use the DAC criteria as the starting set of evaluation lenses and VfI as the judgement architecture.







DAC criterion
VfI interpretation




Relevance
Is the investment aimed at a need, priority, or value proposition that matters?


Coherence
Does the investment fit with other activity, avoid duplication, and create useful synergies?


Effectiveness
Is the investment producing intended results, and for whom?


Efficiency
Are resources being converted into outputs and outcomes well, considering cost, timeliness, quality, and delivery constraints?


Impact
Is the investment creating meaningful wider value, including intended and unintended effects?


Sustainability
Is the value likely to endure after the current investment, delivery model, or funding period?



The synthesis should then add:

context-specific criteria where the DAC set is incomplete;
performance standards for each criterion;
evidence expectations for each standard;
an explicit synthesis process for reaching criterion-level and overall judgements;
a clear account of uncertainty, omitted evidence, and trade-offs.

title: VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
VfI and OECD DAC Relationship
text:
Main Takeaway
The best relationship is nested and complementary. DAC criteria can provide the common evaluation language. VfI can provide the evaluative reasoning needed to judge good resource use.
The resulting evaluation is stronger than either framework used mechanically. It can be comprehensive without being a checklist, and judgement-based without being opaque.

title: Sourcing Testing and Piloting
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on testing and piloting services (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Testing and Piloting Services Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Testing and Piloting
text:
One-Page Summary
The testing and piloting guidance explains how different forms of testing can improve outsourcing and insourcing projects by producing evidence about what works before full implementation. It covers policy trials, proof of concept, scoping phases, agile approaches, innovation partnerships, test-and-learn approaches, and pilots.
For VfM rubric creation, this source is relevant because it makes uncertainty and learning explicit. Testing and piloting can generate evidence for service viability, user needs, requirements, commercial strategy, and implementation risk.

title: Sourcing Testing and Piloting
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance is aimed at practitioners developing testing and pilot approaches. It says testing and piloting should be considered as part of delivery model assessment, commercial strategy, and the project business case.

title: Sourcing Testing and Piloting
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Testing and piloting provide insight and evidence into what works.
Early testing can help understand viability and whether the final delivery model is fit for purpose.
Testing against user needs is essential.
Departments should design, run, and evaluate tests and pilots, then incorporate findings into commercial strategy and market approach.
Multi-stage procurement approaches and financial models may need to reflect testing and piloting.

Inferred VfM implications:

A VfM rubric should reward proportionate learning before irreversible commitment.
Pilot evidence can define realistic standards for effectiveness, efficiency, and risk.
Poor or absent learning can be a VfM weakness when uncertainty is high.

title: Sourcing Testing and Piloting
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Testing and piloting contribution




Effectiveness
Tests whether the service model works for users and intended outcomes before scale-up.


Efficiency
Tests whether learning reduces avoidable implementation failure, rework, or waste.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether pilot costs are proportionate to decision uncertainty and potential value.


Risk and resilience
Tests whether operational, demand, supplier, and implementation risks have been surfaced.


Learning
Tests whether evidence from pilots changes requirements, delivery model, or commercial strategy.

title: Sourcing Testing and Piloting
Sourcing Testing and Piloting
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include testing material where it informs evidence, uncertainty, standards, user value, implementation risk, or staged commitment.
Exclude testing mechanics unless they affect the credibility or use of the learning evidence.

title: Sourcing Testing and Piloting
text:
Limits and Exclusions
Testing evidence may be narrow or context-specific. It should not be treated as proof of full-scale effectiveness unless the pilot design supports that inference.

text:

title: Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on should-cost modelling (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Should Cost Modelling Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
text:
One-Page Summary
The should-cost modelling guidance describes should-cost models as whole-life cost models used to plan, monitor, support decision-making, and drive value for money in public services and public works.
For VfM rubric creation, should-cost modelling is relevant because it provides the cost side of the resource-use judgement. It can inform delivery model assessment, business cases, bid deliverability checks, negotiation, contract management, and monitoring. It also creates evidence for whether prices are plausible, whether cost drivers are understood, and whether low-cost bids are deliverable.

title: Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance is high-level rather than a technical modelling manual. It explains what should-cost models are, when and why contracting authorities should produce them, and key considerations when developing or procuring them.

title: Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Should-cost models describe whole-life cost modelling.
They can range from simple spreadsheets to complex specialist models.
Effective modelling can support planning, monitoring, decision-making, and value for money.
The guidance links modelling to multiple phases, including planning, design, build, populate, review, quality assurance, and use.
Should-cost models can help assess bid deliverability and may sometimes be used in formal evaluation criteria.

Inferred VfM implications:

Should-cost models are evidence, not the VfM judgement itself.
A rubric should test model purpose, assumptions, data quality, assurance, sensitivity, and use in decision-making.
Models are especially relevant to economy, cost-effectiveness, risk, affordability, and deliverability.

title: Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Should-cost contribution




Economy
Tests whether expected costs and cost drivers are understood before procurement.


Efficiency
Tests whether the delivery model or bid converts resources into outputs at plausible cost.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether whole-life cost is weighed against expected benefits and alternatives.


Risk and resilience
Tests whether cost uncertainty, inflation, demand, transition, and supplier risk are visible.


Evidence quality
Tests whether assumptions, data, version control, and quality assurance are adequate.

title: Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include should-cost material where it informs resource use, whole-life cost, alternatives, deliverability, pricing, assumptions, or evidence quality.
Exclude technical spreadsheet mechanics unless they affect whether the cost evidence is credible enough for a rubric judgement.

title: Sourcing Should Cost Modelling
text:
Limits and Exclusions
This note does not summarise the separate should-cost tools and templates. Those tools are better cited as supporting materials where a specific evaluation needs them.

title: Sourcing Playbook
text:
One-Page Summary
The Sourcing Playbook is government guidance on service delivery, including outsourcing, insourcing, mixed economy sourcing, and contracting. It sets out 11 key policies that central government departments are expected to follow when considering service delivery and market engagement.
The direct source claim most relevant to VfM rubric creation is that better sourcing depends on disciplined preparation before going to market: publishing pipelines, understanding markets, validating projects, choosing a delivery model, developing should-cost estimates, testing first-generation outsourcing, setting KPIs, allocating risk appropriately, designing pricing and payment mechanisms, assessing supplier financial standing, and planning for resolution.
The VfM implication is that a sourcing VfM rubric should not start with the contract award result. It should test whether the authority has made a traceable choice about the right delivery model, the right market strategy, the right quality and performance standards, and the right risk and payment design before judging whether the final contract represents good resource use.

title: Sourcing Playbook
Sourcing Playbook
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Sourcing decisions should consider insourcing, outsourcing, and mixed economy models rather than assuming market delivery.
Delivery model assessments should be evidence based and supported by should-cost modelling.
Market health matters because the government can influence supplier markets through pipelines, frameworks, commercial strategies, contract design, and demand aggregation.
Evaluation criteria should avoid a bias toward low-cost bids and should account for quality, social value, and deliverability.
Risk should be allocated appropriately rather than transferred indiscriminately.
Contracts should include KPIs and baselines, but those measures need to reflect the intended service outcomes.

Inferred VfM implications:

Economy is not just price minimisation; it includes commercial discipline, market stewardship, and the avoidance of false economies.
Efficiency includes the quality of the sourcing process, not only the efficiency of the eventual supplier.
Effectiveness depends on whether the chosen delivery model and contract design can plausibly deliver the intended outcomes.
Cost-effectiveness depends on the selected delivery model compared with credible alternatives.
Risk and resilience may need to be explicit rubric criteria or subcriteria for complex services.

title: Sourcing Playbook
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Playbook contribution to rubric design




Economy
Tests whether the authority understood market pricing, cost drivers, competition, and whole-life cost before committing resources.


Efficiency
Tests whether preparation, approval, sourcing, piloting, and contract design avoided avoidable delay, rework, or transaction cost.


Effectiveness
Tests whether requirements, KPIs, baselines, and supplier incentives are aligned with intended service outcomes.


Equity and fairness
Tests whether market engagement, supplier access, and social value considerations support fair and transparent competition.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether the chosen delivery model is justified against in-house, outsourced, hybrid, and other feasible alternatives.


Risk and resilience
Tests whether allocation of operational, financial, performance, and continuity risk supports sustainable delivery.

title: Sourcing Playbook
Sourcing Playbook
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
The playbook is relevant where it helps define what “good” sourcing looks like before, during, and after procurement. It is especially relevant to criteria, standards, evidence sources, trade-offs, alternatives, and accountability.
It should be used as a framework source rather than as evidence that any individual procurement is good VfM. The evaluator still needs programme-specific standards and evidence.

title: Sourcing Playbook
text:
Limits and Exclusions
This note does not summarise every procedural requirement in the playbook. Procedural details are only included where they affect VfM rubric design.
The playbook is also not a complete evaluation method. It identifies commercial practices that should be tested by a VfM rubric, but the rubric still needs evaluative standards, evidence quality checks, and synthesis rules from sources such as the OPM King VfM Guidance and Verian Value for Investment Guidance.

title: Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on using the Sourcing Playbook in conjunction with framework agreements (Cabinet Office and Government Commercial Function 2023).
The main source is:

GOV.UK HTML: Using the Playbook in Conjunction with Framework Agreements

title: Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
text:
One-Page Summary
This guidance clarifies how Sourcing Playbook policy responsibilities are shared between framework providers and calling-off contracting authorities. The central message for VfM rubric creation is that use of a framework does not remove the need for proportionate sourcing discipline.
The source distinguishes framework provider responsibilities, calling-off authority responsibilities, and enabling information that a framework provider can make available. For example, a framework provider may carry out market health assessment before establishing the framework, while a calling-off authority may still need to consider whether the framework is the right route for its specific requirement.

title: Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance applies where departments use framework agreements to meet service or capability needs. It is most relevant to rubric creation where a VfM judgement might otherwise assume that framework use is automatically economical, efficient, or compliant with the Sourcing Playbook.

title: Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Framework providers and calling-off authorities can hold different parts of the evidence base.
Market health and capability assessment may be undertaken at framework level, but call-off authorities may need supplementary assessment for their own requirements.
Framework use should still be considered against the delivery model, market, risk, and value questions raised by the Sourcing Playbook.

Inferred VfM implications:

A VfM rubric should test the fit between the framework and the specific requirement, not just whether a framework route was available.
Frameworks can reduce transaction cost, but they can also limit competition, lock out new suppliers, or weaken requirement-specific evidence if used uncritically.
Evidence ownership matters: some rubric evidence will sit with the framework provider and some with the call-off authority.

title: Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Framework-specific rubric implication




Economy
Test whether framework pricing and competition remain appropriate for the requirement.


Efficiency
Test whether framework use reduced avoidable process cost without skipping essential analysis.


Effectiveness
Test whether the framework can deliver the specific service outcomes and quality thresholds.


Equity and fairness
Test whether supplier access, competition, and transparency are preserved at framework and call-off stages.


Risk and resilience
Test whether framework-level risk controls are sufficient for the criticality of the call-off.

title: Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include framework material where it clarifies accountability, evidence ownership, market effects, supplier access, price discipline, or delivery model fit.
Use framework procedural detail only as supporting context unless it changes the VfM judgement.

title: Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements
text:
Limits and Exclusions
This note does not treat framework compliance as a VfM standard by itself. The relevant VfM question is whether the framework route was suitable and well used for the specific requirement.

title: Sourcing and Consultancy Playbooks for VfM Rubric Creation
text:
Transparent Relevance Rules








Treatment
Rule
Example




Include
The material defines value proposition, resource use, delivery model choice, quality thresholds, criteria, standards, evidence, trade-offs, alternatives, or accountability.
Delivery model assessment criteria, should-cost assumptions, benefit baselines, risk allocation matrix.


Use as supporting context
The material explains process or governance that affects evidence credibility but is not itself a VfM criterion.
Approval routes, record keeping, consultation with specialist teams.


Exclude
The material is procedural detail with no evaluative significance, duplicated elsewhere, or too tool-specific for a generic rubric.
Detailed spreadsheet mechanics, generic contact routes, legal process detail that does not affect judgement.

title: Sourcing and Consultancy Playbooks for VfM Rubric Creation
text:
Candidate Rubric Areas








Candidate area
Core VfM question
Evidence prompts




Economy and commercial discipline
Were inputs, suppliers, consultancy support, and market routes selected at reasonable cost and suitable quality?
Market assessment, should-cost model, pricing rationale, framework route rationale, evaluation report.


Delivery model and alternatives
Was the chosen model justified against credible in-house, outsourced, hybrid, framework, or consultancy alternatives?
Delivery model assessment, options appraisal, business case, cost and non-cost criteria.


Quality and effectiveness
Were specifications, KPIs, evaluation criteria, and benefits aligned with intended outcomes?
Specification, KPI baselines, bid quality criteria, benefits register, review reports.


Cost-effectiveness
Did the value expected or realised justify the whole-life cost and opportunity cost?
Should-cost model, benefits evidence, comparator options, sensitivity analysis, realised performance.


Market fairness and resilience
Did the commercial approach support healthy competition, supplier access, and sustainable provision?
Market health assessment, supplier engagement, framework competition evidence, EFS checks.


Risk allocation and incentives
Were risks allocated to the party best able to manage them, at an efficient price, with incentives aligned to delivery?
Risk allocation matrix, pricing model, payment mechanism, contract monitoring.


Learning and capability
Did the intervention generate reusable knowledge, transfer skills, and reduce avoidable future dependency?
Knowledge transfer plan, capability evidence, reuse records, consultancy close-out evidence.

title: Sourcing and Consultancy Playbooks for VfM Rubric Creation
text:
Candidate Standards for Rubric Drafting
These are candidate standards to adapt, not a final rubric.







Level
Generic standard




Poor
Commercial route, costs, risks, quality expectations, or benefits are weakly defined; evidence cannot support a clear VfM judgement.


Adequate
Core sourcing evidence exists, but important assumptions, trade-offs, or post-award value evidence are incomplete.


Good
Delivery model, cost, market, risk, quality, and benefits evidence are proportionate and clearly linked to the VfM judgement.


Excellent
Evidence is robust, tested, and used adaptively; trade-offs are explicit; benefits, resilience, and learning are monitored through delivery.

Limits and Exclusions
The playbooks should not be used to force every procurement process into a VfM rubric. The rules are selective: a source item is relevant only where it changes what good resource use means, what evidence is needed, how standards should be framed, or how trade-offs should be weighed.
Where a rubric implication is not directly stated in a source note, it should be labelled as an inference and justified by linking back to the relevant source material.

title: Sourcing Benefits Measurement
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on commercial benefits measurement (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Benefits Measurement Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Benefits Measurement
text:
One-Page Summary
The benefits measurement guidance sets minimum requirements for measuring the realisation of commercial benefits in in-scope procurements. It requires commercial benefits and measurement methods to be captured in the benefits register during business case development, initial review after 12 months of delivery, annual reviews thereafter on a comply-or-explain basis, up-to-date benefits reports, and external review for a sample of complex outsourcing contracts.
For VfM rubric creation, this source is important because it links contract delivery to intended commercial benefits. It helps prevent a VfM judgement that stops at procurement award or contract signature.

title: Sourcing Benefits Measurement
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance applies to new outsourcing procurements above the relevant threshold. It focuses on commercial benefits: benefits that materialise during contract implementation as a result of the selected commercial approach.

title: Sourcing Benefits Measurement
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Monitoring benefits realisation is a critical aspect of policy delivery.
Commercial benefits should be identified in the benefits register and measured through mechanisms such as contractual KPIs or internal departmental KPIs.
A 12-month benefits review and subsequent annual reviews should be undertaken as a minimum on a comply-or-explain basis.
Benefits reports should compare baseline, target, and actual performance.
Accountability involves the commercial lead and the senior responsible owner.

Inferred VfM implications:

VfM rubric evidence should include benefits realisation, not only procurement process evidence.
Benefits measurement supports effectiveness and cost-effectiveness criteria.
A strong rubric should test whether benefits were specific, measurable, owned, reviewed, and acted on.

title: Sourcing Benefits Measurement
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Benefits measurement contribution




Effectiveness
Tests whether intended commercial benefits are being delivered during contract implementation.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether realised benefits justify the selected commercial approach and resources used.


Efficiency
Tests whether benefits monitoring identifies avoidable underperformance or management action.


Accountability
Tests whether benefit ownership, reporting, and review are clear.


Evidence quality
Tests whether baseline, target, actuals, and review records are maintained.

title: Sourcing Benefits Measurement
Sourcing Benefits Measurement
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include benefits material where it informs outcome evidence, standards, accountability, or the link between commercial approach and realised value.
Exclude generic benefits-management process unless it changes the VfM evidence base.

title: Sourcing Benefits Measurement
text:
Limits and Exclusions
The source focuses on commercial benefits rather than all strategic policy benefits. A VfM rubric should combine this note with programme theory of change and wider public value evidence.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
This note summarises the OECD DAC evaluation criteria using the OECD’s official evaluation criteria page and the OECD DAC Network on Development Evaluation publications that revised and explained the criteria (OECD 2019, 2021).
The related source pages are:

OECD: Evaluation Criteria
OECD: Better Criteria for Better Evaluation
OECD: Applying Evaluation Criteria Thoughtfully

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
This note summarises the OECD DAC evaluation criteria using the OECD’s official evaluation criteria page and the OECD DAC Network on Development Evaluation publications that revised and explained the criteria (OECD 2019, 2021).
The related source pages are:

OECD: Evaluation Criteria
OECD: Better Criteria for Better Evaluation
OECD: Applying Evaluation Criteria Thoughtfully

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Core Idea
The OECD DAC criteria are a shared framework for judging the merit, worth, or significance of an intervention. They are not a method in themselves. They define the main evaluative questions that an evaluation may need to answer.
The criteria work as complementary lenses. Together, they help evaluators examine whether an intervention is appropriate to the context, fits with other activity, achieves results, uses resources well, produces important wider effects, and leaves benefits that can continue.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
The Six Criteria
The revised OECD DAC criteria are relevance, coherence, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability.

Relevance asks whether the intervention is doing the right things. It examines how far objectives and design respond to needs, policies, priorities, and changing circumstances.
Coherence asks how well the intervention fits. It examines compatibility with other interventions, policies, standards, actors, and systems in the same context.
Effectiveness asks whether the intervention is achieving its objectives. It examines achieved or expected results, including differential results across groups.
Efficiency asks how well resources are being used. It examines whether results are delivered economically and in a timely way.
Impact asks what difference the intervention makes. It examines significant positive or negative, intended or unintended, higher-level effects.
Sustainability asks whether the benefits will last. It examines whether the net benefits of the intervention continue or are likely to continue.

The 2019 revision added coherence as a distinct criterion and updated the other definitions to support more nuanced analysis, including attention to equity, environmental effects, synergies, and trade-offs.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Principles for Use
The OECD gives two principles for applying the criteria. First, they should be applied thoughtfully, with their meaning interpreted in light of the intervention, stakeholders, context, and evaluation questions. Second, their use should depend on the purpose of the evaluation. An evaluation does not need to cover every criterion mechanically.
This means the criteria should be selected and adapted before analysis begins. The evaluation design should explain which criteria matter most, how each criterion is interpreted for the intervention, and what evidence would support a credible judgement.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Practical Implications for VfM Evaluation
For value for money evaluation, the DAC criteria help broaden the judgement beyond cost and impact alone. Efficiency is directly relevant to resource use, but it is only one lens. A low-cost intervention may still represent poor value if it is not relevant, duplicates other work, produces weak or inequitable results, creates harmful wider effects, or leaves benefits that cannot be sustained.
The criteria are especially useful when they are turned into explicit evaluation questions, indicators, evidence standards, or rubrics. This makes the judgement traceable: readers can see how evidence under each criterion contributes to the overall conclusion.
The OECD framing is relevant to definitions of VfM because it treats evaluation criteria as lenses for judging interventions. The criteria are “not a methodology or approach”; they define questions that evaluations need to answer. For VfM, this means efficiency should usually be interpreted alongside relevance, coherence, effectiveness, impact, and sustainability rather than isolated as a narrow resource-use metric.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Limits and Risks
The criteria do not replace evaluation design. They do not decide the method, identify the counterfactual, define evidence quality, or explain how trade-offs should be weighed. Those choices still need to be made explicitly.
A common risk is treating the criteria as a checklist. A stronger evaluation uses them to focus inquiry: selecting the criteria that matter for the decision, defining clear questions, assessing evidence quality, and explaining how mixed findings have been synthesised.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Links
See also the OPM King VfM Guidance note for a rubric-based approach to criteria, standards, synthesis, and judgement.
See also the Magenta Book VfM Evaluation note for UK government guidance on value for money evaluation, including Four Es, monetised analysis, and rubric-based approaches.
