title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
This note summarises the Magenta Book’s guidance on value for money evaluation, with emphasis on the 2026 HTML version of the central guidance and Annex A’s analytical methods (HM Treasury and Evaluation Task Force 2026b, 2026a). It also links the evaluation guidance to the Green Book’s value for money supplementary guidance (HM Treasury 2022).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: The Magenta Book
Magenta Book: Central Government guidance on evaluation
Magenta Book Annex A: analytical methods for use within an evaluation
Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Value for Money

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
This note summarises the Magenta Book’s guidance on value for money evaluation, with emphasis on the 2026 HTML version of the central guidance and Annex A’s analytical methods (HM Treasury and Evaluation Task Force 2026b, 2026a). It also links the evaluation guidance to the Green Book’s value for money supplementary guidance (HM Treasury 2022).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: The Magenta Book
Magenta Book: Central Government guidance on evaluation
Magenta Book Annex A: analytical methods for use within an evaluation
Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Value for Money

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Core Idea
The Magenta Book frames value for money evaluation around a practical question: whether an intervention is a good use of public resources. That question cannot be answered by impact evidence alone. Impact evaluation can estimate what changed, but VfM evaluation asks whether the outcomes, costs, unintended effects, and alternatives justify the use of resources.
The guidance treats VfM as a balanced judgement about public value. Monetised costs and benefits matter, but the judgement should also account for important benefits and costs that cannot be monetised or placed in a benefit-cost ratio.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Relationship to Appraisal
Magenta Book VfM evaluation is closely connected to Green Book appraisal. A standard VfM evaluation compares the costs and benefits achieved by the intervention with the expectations set out in the business case or impact assessment.
A more developed evaluation can go further by comparing different ways of achieving the same strategic objective. This makes VfM evaluation useful for both accountability and learning: it can show whether appraisal assumptions held up in practice and whether future business cases should adjust their forecasts.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Planning Principles
The Magenta Book gives four practical design principles for VfM evaluation:

Plan VfM from the outset, alongside process and impact evaluation.
Define what good value means before the analysis begins.
Link the evaluation back to appraisal forecasts and business case assumptions.
Build from observed data wherever possible, while being clear when forecasts are needed for longer-term benefits or costs.

The design implication is that cost, process, outcome, and contextual data need to be collected deliberately. A late VfM exercise may be forced to rely on incomplete cost data, weak attribution, or assumptions that cannot be tested.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Common Methods
The Magenta Book identifies social cost-benefit analysis as a widely used method when the main costs and benefits can be quantified and monetised. This can produce summary measures such as benefit-cost ratios, and it can compare options with different types of outcomes.
Other related approaches include social cost-effectiveness analysis, cost utility analysis, and social return on investment. These are most useful when the evaluation can measure enough of the intervention’s costs and outcomes to support credible comparison.
The guidance is clear that monetised findings need context. A reported ratio or net present value should sit alongside evidence on major unmonetised benefits, unmonetised costs, limitations, uncertainty, and omitted effects.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Four Es Framework
Where monetisation is incomplete or unsuitable, the Magenta Book describes the Four Es framework as an additional way to structure VfM assessment:

Economy: whether inputs were purchased or obtained at appropriate cost and quality.
Efficiency: whether resources were converted into outputs or outcomes well.
Effectiveness: whether the intervention achieved its intended outcomes.
Equity: whether benefits, costs, and access were distributed fairly.

The Four Es can organise mixed quantitative and qualitative evidence, but they do not usually produce a comparable numerical summary. They are criteria for making a judgement, not a substitute for explaining how evidence has been weighed.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Rubric-Based Approaches
Annex A explicitly recognises rubric-based VfM assessment. A rubric defines criteria and performance standards, often in a table, so evaluators can judge whether observed performance is poor, adequate, good, excellent, or another agreed standard.
The method is especially useful when outcomes are intangible, hard to monetise, or spread across several dimensions of public value. The rubric should be co-designed early, aligned with the Theory of Change, and agreed before evidence is interpreted. This reduces the risk of inventing standards after results are known.
Rubrics do not generate VfM metrics by themselves. Their value is that they provide a transparent way to interpret mixed evidence and synthesize it into an overall evaluative judgement.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Link to OPM King
The Magenta Book’s treatment of rubric-based approaches is closely aligned with the OPM King VfM Guidance. Both approaches treat VfM as explicit evaluative judgement rather than only financial calculation.
The Annex A discussion also cites Julian King’s OPM guide and gives examples of government evaluations using Four Es and rubric-based frameworks. This makes the OPM King approach part of the recognised UK government evaluation methods landscape, especially for interventions where monetisation is difficult or partial.
The Verian Value for Investment Guidance develops a closely related UK public services argument: cost-benefit analysis can be useful evidence, but VfM evaluation often needs wider criteria, mixed evidence, and explicit standards to judge non-monetised public value.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications
For evaluators, the Magenta Book points to a staged workflow:

Start from the decision context and appraisal assumptions.
Decide which benefits and costs can be credibly monetised.
Identify important non-monetised effects that still matter for public value.
Select a method or combined framework that fits the intervention and evidence base.
Define criteria and standards early, especially if using Four Es or rubrics.
Gather empirical cost, process, output, outcome, and distributional evidence.
Report both summary measures and judgement-based findings with limitations.

The most credible VfM evaluations make the judgement traceable. Readers should be able to see how appraisal expectations, observed evidence, monetised analysis, unmonetised effects, uncertainty, and stakeholder-relevant criteria combine into the final conclusion.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Links
See also the Verian Value for Investment Guidance note for a practical account of combining cost-benefit analysis with broader evaluative judgement.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
This note summarises HM Treasury’s Green Book definition of value for money, using the 2026 Green Book and the 2022 Green Book supplementary guidance on value for money (HM Treasury 2026, 2022).
The related source pages are:

HM Treasury: The Green Book (2026)
HM Treasury: Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Value for Money

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
This note summarises HM Treasury’s Green Book definition of value for money, using the 2026 Green Book and the 2022 Green Book supplementary guidance on value for money (HM Treasury 2026, 2022).
The related source pages are:

HM Treasury: The Green Book (2026)
HM Treasury: Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Value for Money

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
One-Page Summary
The Green Book is HM Treasury’s central guidance on appraisal. It provides a structured way to assess the costs, benefits, and risks of different options for achieving government objectives. It is mandatory for UK government departments and arm’s length bodies when developing proposals involving significant public spending.
The Green Book is mainly appraisal guidance, not evaluation guidance. Appraisal happens before approval and implementation; evaluation happens after approval and implementation. The Green Book still matters for evaluation because it requires practitioners to consider from the outset how a proposal will be monitored and evaluated, and because later evaluation should test whether the appraisal’s objectives, costs, benefits, risks, assumptions, and distributional expectations held up in practice. The ROAMEF cycle links rationale, objectives, appraisal, monitoring, evaluation, and feedback.
The core Green Book value concept is social value, also described as public value. Social value covers the benefits, costs, and risks that affect the wellbeing of the UK population. It is not limited to financial returns to government or taxpayers. Material impacts may affect people, places, public bodies, businesses, households, charities, the environment, security, health, justice, climate, distribution, and future wellbeing.
The Green Book defines value for money as a balanced judgement about the optimal use of public resources to achieve a proposal’s objectives. This judgement considers performance against objectives and critical success factors, monetisable social costs and benefits, unmonetisable social costs and benefits, public sector financial impact, distribution across social groups and places, and risk and uncertainty.
This makes value for money broader than choosing the option with the highest benefit-cost ratio. Options that do not achieve the proposal’s objectives cannot represent value for money. Benefit-cost ratios, net present social value, cost-effectiveness ratios, and other economic measures can be important evidence, but they must be interpreted alongside strategic fit, unmonetised effects, affordability, deliverability, distribution, risk, and uncertainty.
The Green Book method starts with rationale and objectives. Practitioners should be clear about the problem or opportunity, why government action is needed, what would happen without intervention, and what specific objectives the proposal is intended to achieve. Critical success factors help screen and judge options by identifying the essential conditions that any worthwhile option must satisfy.
Option appraisal has two broad stages. Longlist appraisal generates a wide range of possible ways to meet the objectives, including business as usual or do minimum where relevant, then filters those options against critical success factors and constraints. Shortlist appraisal compares a manageable set of viable options in more detail, using social cost-benefit analysis or social cost-effectiveness analysis as appropriate.
The Green Book requires appraisal to identify both monetised and unmonetised costs and benefits. Monetisation should be used where credible and proportionate, but important unmonetisable effects should still be recorded, described, and brought into the value for money judgement. The supplementary value for money guidance is especially useful because it shows how decision advice can legitimately prefer an option other than the highest benefit-cost ratio option when objectives, unmonetisable factors, risk, affordability, or strategic fit make another option stronger overall.
Distributional and place-based analysis matter where costs, benefits, access, burdens, or risks fall unevenly across social groups or places. Distribution is therefore not an optional fairness add-on. Where material, it is part of the value for money judgement.
Risk and uncertainty are central to the Green Book approach. Appraisal should identify risks, account for optimism bias where appropriate, test sensitivity to important assumptions, and explain switching values or scenarios where they are useful. A preferred option should not be presented as good value if the judgement depends on fragile assumptions that have not been tested.
Public sector financial impact is distinct from social value. A proposal may create social value while increasing public expenditure, or save public money while creating social costs. Green Book appraisal should make this distinction clear so decision makers understand both the welfare case and the budgetary implications.
For Value for Investment (VfI), the Green Book is best treated as a major source of appraisal logic, option-comparison discipline, economic evidence, and decision-facing presentation standards. It does not by itself create programme-specific VfI criteria, subcriteria, rubrics, or mixed-methods synthesis. Those need to be designed through a VfI approach such as OPM or Verian (King et al. 2023; King and Hurrell 2024).

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
VFI Relevance
The Green Book is directly relevant to VfI because it defines UK central government’s appraisal framing for value for money. It gives evaluators a disciplined source for thinking about objectives, options, social value, costs, benefits, risk, distribution, public sector affordability, and the difference between monetised evidence and the final balanced judgement.
Its practical contribution to VfI is strongest in five areas:

It anchors the VfI value proposition in objectives, critical success factors, public value, and realistic alternatives.
It prevents the VfI judgement from being reduced to a benefit-cost ratio or budget saving.
It supplies evidence prompts for cost, benefit, risk, uncertainty, distribution, and option comparison.
It helps connect ex post evaluation back to ex ante business-case assumptions.
It supports transparent advice to decision makers about why one option or intervention represents better overall use of public resources than another.

The Green Book should not be copied mechanically into a VfI rubric. VfI still needs to decide which criteria matter in the programme context, how those criteria are defined, what subcriteria capture the most important performance story, what standards distinguish poor from good performance, and how mixed evidence will be synthesised into a judgement.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Source-to-VFI Matrix








Green Book material
Direct source contribution
Candidate VfI use




ROAMEF cycle
Links rationale, objectives, appraisal, monitoring, evaluation, and feedback.
Align VfI questions with the original appraisal logic and use evaluation evidence to test appraisal assumptions.


Objectives and critical success factors
Establishes what the proposal must achieve and the conditions options must satisfy.
Define value proposition, strategic fit, effectiveness thresholds, and minimum acceptable performance standards.


Social or public value
Frames value as effects on UK population wellbeing, not just financial return to government.
Broaden criteria beyond cost to include outcomes, quality, distribution, environment, security, resilience, and other public values.


Longlist and shortlist appraisal
Requires realistic options and structured comparison before selecting a preferred option.
Strengthen cost-effectiveness and alternatives subcriteria; test whether the intervention still compares well with credible alternatives.


Monetised costs and benefits
Provides social cost-benefit and cost-effectiveness evidence where valuation is credible.
Use benefit-cost ratios, net present social value, and cost-effectiveness ratios as evidence, not as the whole judgement.


Unmonetised costs and benefits
Requires material effects that cannot be monetised to be recorded and considered.
Build rubric criteria and qualitative evidence prompts for value that is important but hard to price.


Public sector financial impact
Separates budgetary affordability from social value.
Distinguish economy, affordability, and fiscal impact from overall value for money.


Distributional and place-based analysis
Tests how impacts vary across social groups and places where relevant.
Inform equity, access, burden-sharing, and place-based subcriteria.


Risk, uncertainty, optimism bias, sensitivity, and switching values
Requires explicit treatment of uncertain costs, benefits, delivery, and assumptions.
Set standards for confidence, resilience, risk-adjusted judgement, and evidence quality.


Presentation of results and decision advice
Supports balanced advice rather than automatic selection by single metric.
Structure VfI reporting so ratings, trade-offs, uncertainty, and omitted effects are visible to decision makers.


Monitoring and evaluation
Requires evaluation thinking from the outset and learning from previous evaluations.
Link evidence plans to appraisal assumptions, benefits realisation, outturn comparison, and learning loops.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Criteria and Subcriteria Implications
The following mappings are VfI implications inferred from the Green Book and the VfM supplementary guidance. They are not a complete Green Book rubric and should be tailored to the programme, decision stage, and value proposition.








VfI criterion area
Candidate subcriteria informed by the Green Book
Evidence implications




Economy
Resource stewardship; public sector financial impact; affordability; input cost realism; opportunity cost; appropriate treatment of transfers, sunk costs, and wider fiscal effects.
Evidence should include budgets, whole-life cost estimates, financial impacts, cost assumptions, price evidence, opportunity-cost reasoning, and explanation of what is inside or outside the appraisal boundary.


Efficiency
Conversion of resources into outputs and outcomes; option design; delivery route; proportionality; avoidance of avoidable waste, delay, duplication, or excessive administrative burden.
Evidence should show whether the selected option uses resources well compared with feasible alternatives and whether implementation arrangements support timely, proportionate delivery.


Effectiveness
Achievement of objectives; satisfaction of critical success factors; contribution to intended outcomes; quality of benefits; unintended effects.
Evidence should connect outputs and outcomes to the proposal’s objectives, business-case assumptions, theory of change, monitoring data, and evaluation findings.


Equity and distribution
Distribution of costs, benefits, access, risks, and burdens across social groups and places; place-based effects; differential impacts on affected populations.
Evidence should include distributional analysis, place analysis, disaggregated outcome data, stakeholder evidence, and explanation of who gains, who loses, and who bears risk.


Cost-effectiveness
Social value relative to resources used; comparison with credible options; use of cost-benefit analysis or cost-effectiveness analysis where appropriate; treatment of unmonetised value.
Evidence should compare options, report monetised and non-monetised effects together, and explain whether the preferred option remains justified when key assumptions change.


Risk and resilience
Optimism bias; delivery, demand, cost, benefit, and distributional uncertainty; sensitivity; switching values; scenario robustness; risk ownership.
Evidence standards should require transparent assumptions, risk-adjusted estimates, sensitivity tests, and clear explanation of whether uncertainty changes the VfI judgement.


Strategic fit and deliverability
Fit with policy objectives; critical success factors; five-case model alignment; commercial, financial, and management feasibility.
Evidence should show that the option is not only economically attractive but also strategically necessary, affordable, commercially viable, and deliverable.


Evidence quality and assurance
Robustness of appraisal evidence; proportionality; transparency; use of previous evaluation evidence; monitoring and evaluation plan; decision-facing audit trail.
Higher-quality evidence should be documented, proportionate, reviewable, linked to the appraisal case, and capable of supporting later evaluation and learning.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Performance Standards: Design Prompts Only
These prompts translate Green Book appraisal discipline into VfI-style standards. They are not final standards for any specific programme.







Standard level
Green Book-informed prompt




Poor
The proposal or evaluation cannot show clear objectives, credible alternatives, material costs and benefits, distributional effects, or risk assumptions. The VfM claim relies on a single metric, budget saving, or assertion without explaining unmonetised value, uncertainty, or whether objectives are met.


Adequate
The proposal or evaluation identifies objectives, a reasonable option set, main costs and benefits, and key risks, but some unmonetised effects, distributional issues, public sector financial impacts, or sensitivities are weakly evidenced.


Good
The proposal or evaluation compares credible options, explains objectives and critical success factors, presents monetised and unmonetised effects together, distinguishes social value from fiscal impact, and tests important risks and assumptions.


Excellent
The proposal or evaluation gives decision makers a proportionate, transparent, risk-adjusted, and independently reviewable account of the optimal use of public resources, including objectives, alternatives, social value, affordability, distribution, uncertainty, and evidence needed for monitoring, evaluation, and feedback.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Evidence Prompts
Use these prompts when taking detailed notes from the Green Book or when drawing on it for a VfI evidence plan:

What is the problem, opportunity, or rationale for government action?
What objectives define the change the proposal is meant to achieve?
What critical success factors must any acceptable option satisfy?
What is the counterfactual, business-as-usual, or do-minimum position?
What longlist options were considered, and why were options removed?
What shortlist options were appraised in detail, and are they genuinely comparable?
What is the preferred option, and why does it represent the balanced judgement rather than simply the highest benefit-cost ratio?
Which social costs and benefits have been monetised, and how credible are the valuation assumptions?
Which material costs and benefits are unmonetised, and how are they described, weighted, or brought into the judgement?
What public sector financial impacts are expected, and how do they differ from social costs and benefits?
What distributional or place-based effects are material?
Who benefits, who bears costs, who bears risk, and whether any groups or places are affected differently?
What optimism bias, delivery risk, demand risk, cost uncertainty, benefit uncertainty, or distributional uncertainty has been identified?
Which sensitivity tests, switching values, scenarios, or risk-adjusted estimates change the judgement?
What affordability, commercial, financial, management, or delivery constraints affect the preferred option?
What monitoring data, benefits realisation evidence, and evaluation plan are needed to test the appraisal assumptions later?
What previous evaluation evidence informed the appraisal?
What evidence gaps remain, and how should they affect confidence in the VfI judgement?

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Limits and Exclusions
The Green Book is appraisal guidance. It is not a programme-specific criteria framework and it is not a complete VfI method.
Several limits matter for rubric work:

The Green Book defines the central government appraisal approach to value for money, but it does not provide ready-made programme-specific VfI criteria, subcriteria, or performance standards.
It is strongest on objectives, option comparison, social value, economic appraisal, risk, distribution, and decision advice.
It should be paired with the Magenta Book when the task is evaluation rather than appraisal.
It should be paired with OPM or Verian VfI guidance when the task is rubric design, mixed-evidence synthesis, and explicit evaluative judgement.
It should be paired with sector guidance, such as JSP 507 for defence, where domain-specific costs, benefits, options, and risks need interpretation.
A high benefit-cost ratio, low budget cost, or strong affordability case is not by itself a complete VfI judgement.
All candidate subcriteria and standards in this note are design implications unless explicitly described as direct Green Book claims.

title: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics
text:
This synthesis uses the Government Efficiency Framework summary guide and full framework (HM Treasury 2025a, 2025b).
It interprets the GEF through the VfI judgement architecture used in King/OPM and Verian guidance: value proposition, criteria, standards, mixed evidence, synthesis, trade-offs, and an explicit overall resource-use judgement (King et al. 2023; King and Hurrell 2024).
crumbs: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics

title: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics
text:
One-Page Summary
Direct source claims:

The GEF defines and standardises central government efficiency reporting.
It distinguishes technical and allocative efficiency, cash releasing and monetisable non-cash releasing savings, and efficiency from non-efficiency savings.
It requires quality data, baselines, counterfactuals, outturns, netting of costs, no adverse impact on outcomes, sustainability, avoidance of double counting, and documentation that can withstand impartial scrutiny.
It provides efficiency drivers, reporting expectations, maturity levels, benefits realisation guidance, risk requirements, and governance expectations.

Inferred VfI implications:

The GEF can supply criteria and subcriteria for the efficiency and cost-effectiveness parts of a VfI rubric, but it does not supply a complete VfI method.
VfI should provide the judgement architecture: define the value proposition, choose criteria, set standards, combine financial and non-financial evidence, handle trade-offs, and make the final resource-use judgement.
GEF evidence standards are especially useful for distinguishing claimed savings, forecast efficiencies, delivered efficiencies, and independently defensible realised efficiencies.

Candidate rubric material:

Use GEF definitions to test whether an efficiency claim is valid.
Use GEF drivers to identify candidate subcriteria.
Use GEF maturity levels and evidence requirements to design performance standards.
Use GEF reporting and assurance expectations to build evidence prompts.

title: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics
text:
Transparent Use Rules








Rule
Use in VfI rubric creation
Status




Include
Material that defines efficiency, productivity, savings categories, evidence quality, net savings, recurrence, outcome protection, double counting, or assurance.
Directly relevant.


Include
Material on drivers such as procurement, workforce, demand management, digital, estates, prevention, and shared services where it helps specify candidate subcriteria.
Directly relevant as source context; rubric use is inference.


Use as supporting context
Reporting process, maturity levels, and governance arrangements where they inform standards for evidence strength and delivery confidence.
Relevant for standards and prompts.


Use as supporting context
Examples from functional areas, including property, workforce, data, digital, counter fraud, and project delivery.
Useful as examples, not universal criteria.


Exclude
Procedural reporting mechanics that do not affect criteria, standards, evidence, synthesis, or accountability.
Low rubric relevance.


Exclude
Claims of savings caused by cancellation, underspend, demand shortfall, deferral, legislation or tax changes, lower inflation, or cost shifting.
Excluded by GEF unless separate value evidence is provided.


Exclude from final judgement
Treating GEF financial efficiency as a complete public value assessment.
Requires wider VfI, Green Book, and Magenta Book evidence.

GEF summary guide

title: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics
text:
Criteria and Subcriteria Mapping








VfI criterion area
Candidate subcriteria from GEF
Rubric implication




Economy
Market management, competition, procurement, supplier costs, unit cost benchmarking, estates running costs.
Infer: strong performance should show disciplined input costs without hidden quality loss or cost shifting.


Efficiency
Productivity, technical efficiency, service redesign, digital automation, shared services, demand management, workforce deployment.
Infer: strong performance should show more or equal output for the same or lower input.


Effectiveness
No adverse impact on outcomes or strategic priorities; delivery of intended benefits.
Directly supported as a threshold: savings are not efficiency if outcomes are harmed.


Cost-effectiveness
Allocative efficiency, prevention, alternatives, net efficiency, costs to deliver, Green Book cost-effectiveness/cost-benefit links.
Infer: strong performance should show that the chosen resource allocation improves value relative to credible alternatives.


Risk and resilience
Delivery risk, realisation risk, budget control risk, escalation where plans are off track.
Infer: standards should reflect confidence that savings are deliverable, durable, and managed.


Accountability and assurance
Ownership, central datasets, ARAC oversight, independent scrutiny, auditability, documentation.
Infer: higher standards require transparent, reviewable claims and clear accountability.


Learning and capability
Data quality, benefits management, evidence reuse, digital/data process improvement, workforce capability.
Infer: rubric prompts should ask whether efficiency capability improves, not only whether a saving is booked.
crumbs: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics

title: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics
text:
Performance Standards: Design Prompts Only
These are prompts for standard design, not a complete rubric.








Standard level
GEF-informed design prompt
Source status




Poor
Claimed saving is mainly cancellation, underspend, deferral, income increase, or reduced output; baseline and outcome protection are weak.
Inference from GEF exclusions and criteria.


Adequate
Efficiency opportunity is plausible and scoped, with a basic baseline, forecast, owner, and delivery plan.
Inference from maturity levels 0-2.


Good
Methodology is documented; forecast savings are net of costs; output/outcome impacts, risks, and double counting are actively managed.
Inference from GEF criteria, reporting, and risk sections.


Excellent
Realised savings are evidenced with quality data, outturns, sustained recurrence, independent assurance, benefits tracking, and no adverse outcome impact.
Inference from GEF criteria and governance expectations.
crumbs: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics

title: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics
text:
Evidence Prompts
For a VfI-style rubric, the GEF suggests asking:

What is the value proposition: lower cost for the same or better output, higher output for the same cost, or reallocation to a better route to the same outcome?
What type of efficiency is claimed: technical, allocative, cash releasing, or monetisable non-cash releasing?
What baseline is used, and why is it credible?
What counterfactual spending or delivery profile is used, and what assumptions drive it?
What outturn data shows actual realised savings or productivity change?
Are savings gross or net, and what costs to deliver have been included?
What disbenefits, consequential costs, or impacts on other organisations have been tested?
What evidence shows that outputs, outcomes, quality, and strategic priorities have not deteriorated?
Are savings recurring and sustainable, rather than deferred or shifted?
How has double counting been prevented?
What is the maturity level of the efficiency measure?
Who owns the benefit, who validates it, and what independent assurance has been applied?
What risks could prevent realisation, and what escalation route exists?
What non-monetised benefits or harms need Green Book, Magenta Book, or wider VfI evidence?
crumbs: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics

title: Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics
text:
Limits and Exclusions
The GEF is a central government efficiency and savings framework. It is not a complete VfM or VfI evaluation method. Use it to strengthen the efficiency, savings, evidence, and assurance components of a VfI rubric; use VfI guidance for the overall judgement architecture and use the Green Book and Magenta Book for wider appraisal and evaluation evidence.
All rubric implications in this note are inferences unless explicitly described as direct source claims.

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
This note summarises DfT’s value for money indicator collection (Department for Transport 2022). It focuses on what the indicator reports and what it does not report.
The related source page is:

GOV.UK: DfT value for money indicator

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
This note summarises DfT’s value for money indicator collection (Department for Transport 2022). It focuses on what the indicator reports and what it does not report.
The related source page is:

GOV.UK: DfT value for money indicator

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
Core Idea
The DfT value for money indicator reports the proportion of approved project spending that is assessed as high or very high value for money under DfT’s framework. It is a transparency measure about investment decisions, not an evaluation of realised project performance.
This distinction is central. The indicator is based on expected value at the point of approval. It does not show whether schemes later delivered the benefits, costs, distributional effects, or non-monetised impacts assumed in the appraisal.

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
How the Indicator Works
The indicator aggregates approved spending by value for money category. Projects are weighted by approved spending, so large lower-value projects can materially affect the indicator even if many smaller projects are high value.
The category used in the indicator comes from DfT’s appraisal process. The process starts from monetised evidence such as BCRs and then considers impacts, risks, and non-monetised factors before assigning a final category.
The indicator therefore depends on the quality of the underlying appraisal evidence, the consistency of category assignment, and the treatment of uncertainty.

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
What It Does Not Show
The indicator should not be read as evidence of achieved value for money. It does not compare forecast benefits with realised benefits after implementation. It also does not show whether optimism bias, demand forecasts, cost risks, or non-monetised impacts were borne out in practice.
This makes it useful for understanding DfT’s appraisal and decision pipeline, but limited for evaluation. A separate evaluation would be needed to assess whether the approved portfolio actually delivered high or very high value once implemented.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
This note summarises two DfT value for money assessment examples: Integrated Transport Block funding and major bus-related schemes (Department for Transport 2016a, 2016b).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: Integrated transport block funding: value for money assessment
GOV.UK: Value for money assessment for major bus-related schemes

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
This note summarises two DfT value for money assessment examples: Integrated Transport Block funding and major bus-related schemes (Department for Transport 2016a, 2016b).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: Integrated transport block funding: value for money assessment
GOV.UK: Value for money assessment for major bus-related schemes

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Core Idea
These examples show DfT applying value for money logic to portfolios or groups of transport schemes rather than to a single business case. They are useful because they expose the practical compromises in aggregating appraisal evidence across many local or modal interventions.
The examples are still primarily appraisal-based. They estimate approximate value from available scheme evidence, costs, benefits, assumptions, and sensitivity tests. They are not full ex post evaluations of realised value.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Integrated Transport Block
The Integrated Transport Block assessment examined local authority capital funding for smaller transport improvement schemes. The source is useful because small schemes often have weaker appraisal, monitoring, and evaluation evidence than major projects.
The assessment therefore has to work with uneven local evidence. It uses survey evidence, available appraisal material, monitoring or evaluation evidence where present, and sensitivity tests to estimate approximate VfM.
For evaluators, the important lesson is that evidence systems matter. If local bodies do not collect consistent data on costs, outputs, outcomes, and benefits, later VfM assessment becomes dependent on assumptions and partial evidence.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Major Bus-Related Schemes
The major bus-related schemes assessment reviewed schemes approved under funds including Local Majors, Local Sustainable Transport Fund, and Better Bus Areas. It reported an aggregate BCR and translated that into a DfT value for money judgement.
The report is explicit that only monetised benefits and costs are included in the aggregate analysis, while non-monetised benefits and costs are handled qualitatively in the wider appraisal process. This is a useful example of the boundary between monetised portfolio metrics and broader VfM judgement.
For evaluation, the key question is whether the expected benefits from bus priority, interchange, reliability, passenger growth, decongestion, and wider impacts were realised after implementation.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
Both examples can be used as starting points for retrospective evaluation. The evaluator would need to reconstruct the original appraisal assumptions, gather delivery and outcome evidence, and compare expected and realised value.
Where monetised data are weak, a structured criteria or rubric approach may be needed. The OPM King VfM Guidance is useful here because it gives a way to combine monetised evidence, qualitative evidence, standards, and judgement transparently.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s Transport Analysis Guidance (TAG) landing page and the role of the Transport Appraisal and Strategic Modelling division (Department for Transport 2025, 2026).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: Transport analysis guidance
DfT: Transport appraisal and strategic modelling division

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s Transport Analysis Guidance (TAG) landing page and the role of the Transport Appraisal and Strategic Modelling division (Department for Transport 2025, 2026).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: Transport analysis guidance
DfT: Transport appraisal and strategic modelling division

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Core Idea
TAG is DfT’s transport-specific analytical system for modelling and appraisal. It gives practitioners guidance on defining problems, developing options, building transport models, estimating impacts, and presenting appraisal evidence for business cases.
For value for money work, TAG is the technical base. It supplies the methods and data assumptions that feed the DfT VfM Framework and the economic dimension of the DfT Transport Business Case.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Evaluation Practitioner Guidance
TAG includes guidance for the evaluation practitioner. This matters because DfT treats evaluation as part of the transport appraisal process rather than a disconnected afterthought.
The implication is that evaluation should be planned early enough to collect baseline data, monitor delivery, and compare realised effects with the assumptions used in appraisal. Evaluation can then improve future appraisal by testing demand forecasts, cost assumptions, behaviour change assumptions, benefit realisation, and the treatment of non-monetised impacts.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Analytical Assurance
TASM maintains TAG, develops value for money guidance, scrutinises value for money advice submitted to ministers and DfT investment boards, and oversees analytical assurance. This gives the guidance an institutional role: TAG is both a technical method base and a quality-assurance standard for decision advice.
For evaluators, the assurance role is important because weaknesses in modelling, data, or assumptions can become evaluation questions. A good evaluation can ask whether the appraisal method was proportionate, transparent, and empirically supported.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
TAG is strongest as appraisal guidance. It supports ex ante decisions by estimating expected costs and benefits before delivery. Evaluation uses a different evidence posture: it asks what happened, why, for whom, at what cost, and how this compares with expectations.
The link between the two is learning. TAG assumptions should not be treated as settled truths. They are hypotheses that can be tested through monitoring, process evaluation, impact evaluation, and value for money evaluation.
This connects TAG to the Magenta Book VfM Evaluation, which gives central government guidance on using evaluation evidence to judge actual value after implementation.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
This note summarises the Cabinet Office and Government Commercial Function’s The Consultancy Playbook (Cabinet Office and Government Commercial Function 2022).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: The Consultancy Playbook

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
This note summarises the Cabinet Office and Government Commercial Function’s The Consultancy Playbook (Cabinet Office and Government Commercial Function 2022).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: The Consultancy Playbook

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
One-Page Summary
The Consultancy Playbook gives government guidance on commissioning and engaging consultants more effectively, achieving better outcomes and value for money, and supporting Civil Service capability through the creation of knowledge and skills.
For VfM rubric creation, the playbook is relevant because consultancy value is often not captured by simple deliverable completion. A credible rubric should test why external advice is needed, whether the delivery model is justified, whether evaluation criteria and pricing are aligned with outcomes, whether government acts as an effective client, and whether knowledge generation and transfer reduce future dependence on consultants.

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
Purpose and Scope
The playbook applies to central government departments and arm’s-length bodies going to market for consultancy services. It defines consultancy broadly as advice to fill a knowledge gap and distinguishes that from staff substitution or routine service delivery.

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Consultancy can bring valuable expertise, but government should access the right skills at the right time and in the right way.
Consultancy should be used to fill specific skills and knowledge gaps.
The playbook emphasises right-at-the-start decisions, going to market, and maximising value throughout the engagement.
It covers delivery model choice, setting procurements up for success, market engagement, specifications, evaluation criteria, routes to market, pricing and payment mechanisms, bid evaluation, spend data, client effectiveness, and knowledge generation and transfer.
Knowledge generation and transfer are central to reducing reliance on external consultants.

Inferred VfM implications:

A consultancy VfM rubric should test the justification for external advice before testing supplier performance.
Effectiveness includes usefulness of advice for decisions and implementation, not only production of reports.
Learning and capability should be explicit criteria or subcriteria for consultancy engagements.
Economy should consider whether consultancy scope, route to market, and pricing are proportionate to the knowledge gap.

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Consultancy playbook contribution




Economy
Tests whether consultancy was necessary, scoped tightly, and priced proportionately.


Efficiency
Tests whether government acted as an effective client and avoided poor handoffs, unclear requirements, or duplicated effort.


Effectiveness
Tests whether advice, analysis, or support improved decisions, delivery, policy, or organisational outcomes.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether external support was justified against internal delivery, upskilling, or alternative routes.


Learning and capability
Tests whether knowledge and skills were generated, transferred, captured, and reused.


Equity and fairness
Tests transparent market engagement and supplier access, including SMEs where relevant.

title: Consultancy Playbook
Consultancy Playbook
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include consultancy playbook material where it informs necessity, alternatives, scope, outcome quality, client capability, knowledge transfer, evidence, or standards.
Exclude generic consultancy process where it does not affect VfM judgement.

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
Limits and Exclusions
The playbook does not provide a complete evaluation framework for every consultancy assignment. A rubric still needs programme-specific standards for what counts as useful knowledge, improved decision-making, or sustained capability.

title: Consultancy Playbook
text:
Links
See also the Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer, Sourcing Bid Evaluation, and Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment notes.
Return to the notes overview.

text:

Value for money evaluation

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on knowledge and skills generation, transfer, and sharing (Cabinet Office 2022).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Knowledge and Skills: Generation, Transfer and Sharing Guidance Note

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on knowledge and skills generation, transfer, and sharing (Cabinet Office 2022).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Knowledge and Skills: Generation, Transfer and Sharing Guidance Note

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
One-Page Summary
The guidance note explains how departments should incorporate knowledge generation, knowledge and skills transfer, and sharing across consultancy engagements. It defines knowledge as a human or organisational asset enabling effective decisions and action in context, and skills as learned capacity to perform a task to a specified expectation.
For VfM rubric creation, this source is important because consultancy value should include reusable knowledge and internal capability, not only the immediate contracted output. It supports rubric standards about whether knowledge transfer is planned at the start, embedded in procurement and controls, specified in the contract, delivered during the assignment, captured, and shared for reuse.

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance applies to consultancy assignments and associated spend. It supports the Consultancy Playbook’s aim of making knowledge generation and transfer a default expectation.

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Government aims to use and reuse knowledge that it creates or that is created on its behalf through consultancy and associated spend.
The default expectation is that consultancy assignments should consider knowledge generation, transfer, and sharing.
Procurement and controls processes should test knowledge generation and sharing.
Departments should frame knowledge and skills transfer at the start, consider previous knowledge, and engage relevant internal capability such as HR and workforce planning.

Inferred VfM implications:

Knowledge transfer is not an optional add-on; it can be a VfM criterion for consultancy engagements.
A rubric should test whether transfer is specific, resourced, observable, and reusable.
Evidence should distinguish deliverable handover from actual capability development.

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Knowledge and skills contribution




Effectiveness
Tests whether the consultancy produced knowledge that improved decisions or action.


Efficiency
Tests whether reusable knowledge reduced duplication and future dependency.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether the assignment created lasting capability relative to its cost.


Learning and capability
Tests whether transfer, capture, sharing, and reuse were planned and evidenced.


Evidence quality
Tests whether knowledge outputs and skills outcomes are observable, not merely asserted.

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include knowledge and skills material where it informs capability, sustainability, learning, effectiveness, reuse, and future dependency.
Exclude generic knowledge management terminology unless it helps define a standard or evidence requirement.

title: Consultancy Knowledge and Skills Transfer
text:
Limits and Exclusions
Knowledge transfer evidence can be weak if it only records that a product was delivered. A VfM rubric should look for evidence that people or teams can use the knowledge after the consultancy ends.

text:
Links
See also the Consultancy Playbook, OPM King VfM Guidance, and Verian Value for Investment Guidance notes.

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
This note summarises Welde, Engebo, Gulichsen, Prebensen, and Volden’s 2025 article on ex-post evaluation of the Norwegian Navy’s logistics support vessel HNoMS Maud (Welde et al. 2025).
The related source page is:

Taylor & Francis: Evaluating defence projects - success may be a multidimensional measure
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
This note summarises Welde, Engebo, Gulichsen, Prebensen, and Volden’s 2025 article on ex-post evaluation of the Norwegian Navy’s logistics support vessel HNoMS Maud (Welde et al. 2025).
The related source page is:

Taylor & Francis: Evaluating defence projects - success may be a multidimensional measure
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Core Idea
The article uses HNoMS Maud to show why defence project success and value for money cannot be judged only by delivery time, budget, and technical scope. A defence acquisition can have weak delivery performance but still produce important operational and strategic value. Conversely, a project can be delivered efficiently while failing to meet long-term defence needs.
The central contribution is a worked ex-post evaluation of a major defence acquisition using a DAC-inspired framework. The framework links the familiar project management question of whether the project was delivered efficiently to broader questions about whether the delivered capability is useful, relevant, sustainable, and valuable.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Case Background
HNoMS Maud is a logistics and support vessel acquired for the Royal Norwegian Navy. The project arose from a combination of NATO and national needs. NATO’s 2002 Prague Capabilities Commitment emphasised deployable logistics support, while Norway’s new frigates needed support to operate beyond coastal duties.
The project objective was to acquire a logistics and support capability that would increase the endurance, flexibility, and mobility of Norwegian naval forces. The Norwegian Parliament approved the project in 2009. The contract was awarded to Daewoo Shipbuilding & Marine Engineering in South Korea in 2013, with expected delivery in September 2016.
The vessel was delivered after a turbulent construction period and arrived in Norway in 2019. It was not operationally mature until later, with continuing technical issues affecting systems such as replenishment-at-sea equipment and the Integrated Platform Management System.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Evaluation Design
The evaluation was conducted four years after completion to allow benefits to begin materialising. It used document review, project accounts, interviews, conversations with key participants, and an on-site vessel inspection at Haakonsvern Naval Base.
The evaluation is both summative and formative. It judges the completed project, but its main purpose is learning for future defence acquisitions. This matters because ex-post evaluations of defence projects are rare, partly because of classified information and partly because project evaluation is often less systematic than ex-ante appraisal.
The authors use a goal-oriented evaluation methodology. Actual outcomes are compared with the goals formulated in the project’s business case. This makes the business case important: if goals are incomplete or poorly specified, the evaluation can expose that weakness.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
DAC-Inspired Criteria
The framework is inspired by the OECD DAC criteria, but it is adapted to public project success. It uses six criteria grouped across three levels of success.








Level
Criterion
Evaluation question




Operational
Efficiency
Was the project implemented efficiently in terms of cost, time, and quality?


Tactical
Effectiveness
Were the agreed user goals attained, and did the project contribute to them?


Strategic
Relevance
Is there a need for what the project delivers, judged against national priorities and stakeholder preferences?


Strategic
Sustainability
Are the benefits likely to persist through the vessel’s lifetime, with acceptable long-run financial, environmental, and social impacts?


Strategic
Other impacts
What positive or negative consequences beyond agreed outcomes can be attributed to the project?


Strategic
Benefit-cost efficiency
Is willingness to pay for what the project delivers proportionate to cost, where cost-benefit analysis is feasible?



This is not a mechanical checklist. The criteria create a structured route from project delivery to military utility and societal value. The article’s most useful VfM lesson is that efficiency is necessary but insufficient: the evaluator must ask whether the delivered capability remains relevant and useful in a changing strategic context.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Operational Success
Operational success concerns whether the project organisation converted resources into the delivered vessel efficiently.
On time, the project performed poorly. The contract specified delivery in September 2016, but the vessel was delayed by more than two years and only reached Norway in 2019. From a product-readiness perspective, the delay was longer because the vessel was not fully operational until later. This distinction is important: handover is not the same as having a usable capability.
The delay was driven by a mix of project and supplier risks. The South Korean shipyard faced severe financial and management problems after the oil price crisis, including corruption proceedings against senior management. The project also faced cultural and language barriers, geographical distance, time-zone separation, and a high turnover of project managers.
On cost, the judgement is more nuanced. In nominal terms, final cost was far above the original budget. Adjusted using the defence index, the project was moderately over budget. Adjusted for both inflation and exchange-rate compensation, the final cost was below the maximum budget. The case shows that cost performance in defence procurement can depend heavily on the baseline and indexation method used.
On quality, the vessel was eventually considered useful and well designed, but the achieved quality was below original aspirations because requirements had been cut after the first tender round. Continuing issues with the Integrated Platform Management System are especially important because Maud relies on a relatively small crew and high automation.
The operational judgement is therefore mixed: late delivery and technical problems, but broadly controlled costs after exchange-rate compensation and eventual delivery of a capable vessel.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Tactical Success
Tactical success concerns whether the vessel achieves user-level goals for the Navy and allied operations.
The evaluation finds that Maud is a useful national and international operational resource. Its replenishment-at-sea capability allows Norwegian and allied vessels to maintain readiness and endurance without returning to port. It is built to NATO standards, making it compatible with allied vessels.
The vessel has been deployed with NATO’s Standing Maritime Group 1, and informants reported strong demand for its services. The crewing model was also expanded from one crew to two, increasing potential sailing days substantially.
Maud supports the Navy’s long-term endurance by transporting and transferring fuel, food, ammunition, materiel, and personnel. However, its full potential is constrained by wider capability dependencies. The vessel was designed to support helicopter operations, but Norway’s failure to acquire NH90 helicopters means neither Maud nor the frigates have the intended helicopter capability.
The vessel can also support other branches of the Armed Forces and civil emergency authorities, though not to the extent originally intended after requirements were reduced. Its hospital capability, container capacity, crane, and logistics functions could be valuable in some humanitarian or emergency scenarios.
The tactical judgement is generally positive: the vessel provides real operational value, especially for NATO and offshore naval activity, but some benefits depend on complementary capabilities and operating patterns.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Strategic Success
Strategic success asks whether Maud remains relevant to defence needs and whether its long-term benefits justify the investment.
The article finds a strong international strategic rationale. Norway relies on NATO collective security, and Maud allows Norway to contribute a valuable logistics capability to allied maritime operations. The vessel helps Norway strengthen its NATO profile and support operations that matter to alliance security.
The national strategic case is less clear. Norway’s Navy is small, and Maud’s value depends on a continued commitment to offshore surface operations. If future naval strategy shifted toward coastal operations, the need for Maud would weaken. The vessel’s long-term relevance is therefore tied to future fleet structure, especially whether frigates or equivalent ocean-going vessels are retained.
The evaluation also identifies wider learning benefits. The project gave Norway experience in large international procurement, especially from a South Korean supplier, and highlighted the importance of culture, language, supplier governance, and payment incentives.
From a societal value perspective, conventional cost-benefit analysis is difficult because defence benefits are hard to monetise. The authors therefore frame value more broadly: Maud contributes to protecting the nation, insuring against uncertain future threats, and supporting global influence and international security. Its strongest value-for-society argument is the international dimension.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
VfM Lessons
The case is valuable for VfM evaluation because it shows how a criteria framework can make a complex judgement transparent.
First, delivery performance and value are related but distinct. Maud was late and technically difficult, yet it may still deliver important long-term value if it remains central to Norwegian and NATO operations.
Second, programme theory matters. The article argues that a theory of change should test the logic from outputs to user effects and wider impacts. In this case, the project goals did not clearly state the NATO contribution, even though NATO needs were central to the project’s origin and later value.
Third, front-end appraisal should consider genuinely different options. The appraisal of what became Maud appears to have examined variants of a single vessel concept rather than alternative ways of strengthening military capability.
Fourth, VfM in defence depends on complementary systems. Maud’s value is affected by frigates, helicopters, crewing, operating budgets, NATO demand, and future fleet structure. Evaluating the vessel in isolation would understate these dependencies.
Fifth, international procurement risk needs explicit treatment. Culture, language, supplier financial health, governance, and payment terms all affected delivery. The authors recommend payment profiles that give suppliers stronger incentives for timely, high-quality delivery.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Relationship to DAC Criteria
The paper is a useful companion to the OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria note because it shows the criteria being adapted to a defence acquisition rather than applied in development assistance.
The adaptation is important. The article does not simply reproduce the revised DAC set of relevance, coherence, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability. Instead, it uses a public-project-success model derived from DAC thinking. “Other impacts” covers wider positive and negative consequences, while “benefit-cost efficiency” makes explicit the VfM question of whether benefits are proportionate to cost.
The paper also complements OPM King VfM Guidance and Magenta Book VfM Evaluation, because it demonstrates the same broad principle: credible VfM evaluation often requires structured qualitative judgement alongside cost, delivery, and performance evidence.
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
section: Practical Implications for Defence VfM Evaluation
crumbs: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation

title: Defence Logistics Vessel DAC VfM Evaluation
text:
Links
See also the OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria, Magenta Book VfM Evaluation, and OPM King VfM Guidance notes.
See also the Defence VfM Guidance report for broader defence value for money framing.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s transport business case guidance (Department for Transport 2022). It focuses on how the business case process uses appraisal evidence, Transport Analysis Guidance, the Green Book, and DfT’s value for money framework.
The related source page is:

GOV.UK: Transport business case guidance

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s transport business case guidance (Department for Transport 2022). It focuses on how the business case process uses appraisal evidence, Transport Analysis Guidance, the Green Book, and DfT’s value for money framework.
The related source page is:

GOV.UK: Transport business case guidance

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Core Idea
DfT uses the five-case model to structure transport investment decisions. Value for money sits mainly in the economic dimension, but the guidance is clear that the five dimensions are connected. A proposal can only be strong if it has strategic fit, economic value, commercial viability, financial affordability, and credible delivery arrangements.
The business case is therefore not just a container for a BCR. It is an iterative decision document that should become more accurate as evidence develops from strategic outline case to outline business case to full business case.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Business Case Dimensions
The five dimensions create a practical frame for VfM:

Strategic dimension: whether the proposal responds to a real transport problem and fits national, regional, local, and organisational priorities.
Economic dimension: whether the proposal is the best choice for maximising social welfare, using options appraisal and DfT’s value for money framework.
Commercial dimension: whether the market, procurement route, and supply-side capacity can deliver the proposal.
Financial dimension: whether the proposal is affordable within the relevant budgets.
Management dimension: whether the proposal can be delivered and managed from outputs through to outcomes.

This matters for VfM evaluation because weak delivery, affordability, or commercial assumptions can undermine expected value even when the appraisal ratio looks strong.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
section: Role of TAG and the VfM Framework
text:
Role of TAG and the VfM Framework
The guidance positions TAG as the detailed transport appraisal and modelling guidance used to produce consistent evidence for business cases. DfT’s value for money framework then explains how appraisal outputs are converted into a VfM assessment and communicated to decision makers.
The economic dimension should include economic, environmental, and social impacts. It should use monetised evidence where possible, but also account for qualitative and quantitative evidence where monetisation is not credible or complete. This aligns with the Green Book VfM Guidance and the DfT VfM Framework.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Strategic Coherence and Distribution
The 2022 guidance reflects the Green Book Review emphasis on strategic coherence, distributional analysis, and place-based impacts. This is important for transport because schemes often have uneven effects across people, places, transport users, non-users, businesses, and future generations.
For evaluation, this means that the business case should not be treated only as a forecast of aggregate benefits. It is also a record of whose outcomes mattered, where benefits and costs were expected to fall, and which strategic objectives the intervention was supposed to advance.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
The business case sets the expected value story. Evaluation tests that story.
The management dimension is especially important because it should describe how outputs become outcomes and how benefits will be realised. A later VfM evaluation can use the business case to ask:

Were the original objectives and critical success factors still relevant?
Were the short-listed options and counterfactual assumptions reasonable?
Did costs, benefits, risks, and delivery assumptions hold up?
Were non-monetised and distributional effects tracked after approval?
Did commercial, financial, or management issues affect achieved value?

The Magenta Book VfM Evaluation provides the stronger evaluation framing for this comparison between expected and realised value.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Links
See also the DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation, DfT VfM Framework, and DfT VfM Case Examples notes.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s value for money framework and the role of the Transport Appraisal and Strategic Modelling division in maintaining value for money guidance (Department for Transport 2025, 2026).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: DfT value for money framework
DfT: Transport appraisal and strategic modelling division

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s value for money framework and the role of the Transport Appraisal and Strategic Modelling division in maintaining value for money guidance (Department for Transport 2025, 2026).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: DfT value for money framework
DfT: Transport appraisal and strategic modelling division

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
Core Idea
DfT’s framework is an appraisal framework for judging whether expected costs are justified by expected benefits. It is designed for decision advice on proposed transport interventions, especially where ministers and investment boards need a clear value for money statement.
The framework sits inside the wider government appraisal system. It draws on the Green Book VfM Guidance, uses DfT’s Transport Analysis Guidance for transport-specific appraisal methods, and feeds the economic dimension of the DfT Transport Business Case.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
VfM Metrics
The framework starts from monetised appraisal. Benefit-cost ratios (BCRs), net present values, present value of benefits, and present value of costs are central because they give decision makers a disciplined way to compare expected social benefits with expected resource costs.
DfT also recognises that a single monetised ratio is incomplete. Transport proposals can have important environmental, social, place-based, distributional, safety, reliability, resilience, and landscape effects that may not be fully monetised. The VfM judgement therefore needs to combine monetised evidence with qualitative and quantitative evidence on non-monetised impacts.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
VfM Categories
DfT communicates results through value for money categories. The category is not simply the initial BCR band. Analysts are expected to consider non-monetised impacts, risk, uncertainty, sensitivity tests, and the robustness of the analysis before recommending a final category.
This makes the category a structured judgement. The BCR provides the starting point, but the final judgement should explain whether other effects strengthen, weaken, or materially change the initial conclusion.

title: DfT VfM Framework
DfT VfM Framework
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
The DfT framework is mainly ex ante. It guides decisions before schemes are approved and implemented. It does not itself provide a full method for ex post value for money evaluation.
However, it creates a clear evaluation agenda. If appraisal expects benefits from journey time savings, reliability, safety, mode shift, carbon reduction, local growth, or distributional effects, later evaluation should test whether those benefits were delivered and whether the appraisal assumptions were credible.
This links directly to the Magenta Book VfM Evaluation note. The Magenta Book is more explicit about evaluating actual costs, outcomes, unintended effects, and alternatives after implementation. DfT’s framework supplies the original appraisal expectations that evaluation can test.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
Practical Implications
For transport evaluators, the framework is useful as a baseline for reconstructing the original value proposition:

Identify the original VfM category and its monetised basis.
Separate monetised impacts from non-monetised impacts.
Trace the assumptions that drove the BCR or net present value.
Check whether sensitivity tests covered the risks that later mattered.
Compare expected and realised costs, benefits, and distributional effects.
Record lessons for future TAG parameters, optimism bias, and appraisal practice.

Where effects cannot be monetised convincingly, evaluators may need a rubric-based or criteria-based synthesis. That is where the OPM King VfM Guidance complements DfT’s category framework.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
