title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
This source note summarises HM Treasury’s Government Efficiency Framework (GEF) Summary, Key Definitions and Criteria Guide, updated 24 November 2025 (HM Treasury 2025).
The source page is Government Efficiency Framework (GEF) Summary, key definitions and criteria guide.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
This source note summarises HM Treasury’s Government Efficiency Framework (GEF) Summary, Key Definitions and Criteria Guide, updated 24 November 2025 (HM Treasury 2025).
The source page is Government Efficiency Framework (GEF) Summary, key definitions and criteria guide.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
One-Page Summary
Direct source claims:

The GEF sets common standards and definitions so central government organisations can categorise, hold, and report efficiency data consistently.
It defines efficiency as spending less to achieve the same or greater outputs, or achieving higher outputs with the same spending. Cost reductions intended to achieve less are non-efficiency savings.
Productivity gains count as efficiency only where outputs or outcomes stay the same or increase.
The framework distinguishes technical efficiency, allocative efficiency, cash releasing efficiency savings, and monetisable non-cash releasing efficiency savings.
Reportable efficiency savings should be calculated with quality data, net of costs and disbenefits, sustainable and recurring, free from double counting, clear in nature, and verifiable by an impartial third party.
Efficiencies should not adversely affect performance, outcomes, another department, or local government.

Inferred VfI implications:

The summary is useful for rubric criteria on resource use, productivity, output or outcome protection, evidence quality, sustainability, and accountability.
It helps define the boundary between genuine value improvement and simple budget reduction.
It supports performance standards that reward demonstrable, durable, and evidenced efficiency rather than asserted or temporary savings.

Candidate rubric material:

Ask whether claimed efficiencies preserve or improve outputs and outcomes.
Ask whether savings are calculated from a credible baseline, counterfactual, outturn data, costs to deliver, and disbenefits.
Distinguish cash-releasing savings from non-cash benefits and from non-efficiency savings.
Treat cancellation, deferral, cost shifting, and reduced ambition as weak or invalid evidence of VfI unless justified through separate value arguments.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
Purpose and Scope
Direct source claims:
The summary guide is for accounting officers, finance directors, chief operating officers, budget holders, senior responsible owners, and finance or project teams involved in monitoring, delivery, appraisal, and evaluation of efficiency savings in government.
The guide is a central government efficiency and savings framework. It is not presented as a complete evaluation method for public value, distribution, ethics, or broader social outcomes.
Inferred VfI implications:
For VfI rubric creation, the guide is best used as a technical source on efficiency claims and evidence standards. It should sit alongside broader VfI, Green Book, and Magenta Book sources when the rubric needs to make an overall resource-use judgement.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
Key Concepts








Concept
Direct source claim
Rubric relevance




Efficiency
Spend less for the same or greater outputs, or deliver higher outputs for the same spend.
Defines the basic test for an efficiency claim.


Productivity
Output per unit input; productivity only counts as efficiency if outputs or outcomes stay the same or increase.
Prevents false positives where activity becomes cheaper but value falls.


Technical efficiency
Doing something better, faster, or for less.
Useful for economy and operational efficiency subcriteria.


Allocative efficiency
Reallocating resources to a wholly different way of achieving the same or improved outcome.
Useful for alternatives, prevention, and resource allocation subcriteria.


Cash releasing saving
A direct reduction in spending on an activity, project, programme, or policy.
Supports evidence prompts on realised budget impact.


Monetisable non-cash releasing saving
A monetisable efficiency benefit without a direct spending reduction.
Supports productivity and capacity-use evidence prompts.


Non-efficiency saving
A reduction in spend because less output or a different outcome is intended.
Supports exclusion rules for rubric evidence.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
VfI-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Quality data is central to calculating and reporting efficiencies.
Efficiency claims should be net of costs and disbenefits.
Savings should not create unplanned consequential costs elsewhere in government.
Savings should be sustainable and should not defer or reallocate costs into later years.
Savings should be scored once and avoid double counting.
The calculation and nature of savings should be readily understood, reasonable, verifiable, auditable, and well evidenced.

Inferred VfI implications:
These concepts translate into VfI rubric dimensions on evidential credibility, durability, whole-system effects, and accountability. They also support explicit standards for poor, adequate, good, and excellent performance by separating asserted savings from realised, independently defensible efficiency gains.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria








VfI design component
Candidate use
Source status




Criteria
Include efficiency, productivity, outcome protection, and evidence quality.
Inference from GEF definitions and reporting criteria.


Subcriteria
Baseline quality, counterfactual, net savings, recurrence, double-counting control, and audit trail.
Inference from source criteria.


Performance standards
Higher standards should require realised savings, maintained outcomes, transparent methodology, and independent assurance.
Inference from source criteria.


Evidence
Require baseline cost, outturn spend, output or outcome data, cost-to-deliver, disbenefits, and documentation.
Directly supported for financial efficiency claims.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
Evidence Useful for Rubric Creation

Baseline cost or resource-use data.
Counterfactual spending profile, including assumptions.
Outturn spending and delivery data.
Evidence that outputs or outcomes were maintained or improved.
Calculation of gross and net savings.
Costs to deliver the efficiency.
Disbenefits and consequential costs.
Sustainability or recurrence evidence.
Double-counting checks across organisations or functions.
Documentation suitable for review by the NAO, GIAA, or independent auditors.

title: Government Efficiency Framework Summary
text:
Limits and Exclusions
The summary guide is not a complete VfI evaluation architecture. It focuses on efficiency and financial reporting, especially monetisable efficiency. It does not replace Green Book appraisal, Magenta Book evaluation, or VfI rubric design.
Exclude or de-prioritise summary material where it is only a reporting instruction without evaluative significance. Use the full GEF note for more detailed treatment of data, maturity, governance, benefits realisation, and examples.

text:

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
This source note summarises HM Treasury’s The Government Efficiency Framework, updated 24 November 2025 (HM Treasury 2025).
The source page is The Government Efficiency Framework.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
This source note summarises HM Treasury’s The Government Efficiency Framework, updated 24 November 2025 (HM Treasury 2025).
The source page is The Government Efficiency Framework.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
One-Page Summary
Direct source claims:

The GEF is a standard approach for tracking, monitoring, oversight, and reporting of efficiency savings across central government.
It gives definitions, reporting standards, best-practice reporting processes, and guidance on reporting efficiency savings to HM Treasury.
It focuses primarily on productivity and efficiency where they can be measured in financial terms.
It identifies two types of efficiency: technical efficiency and allocative efficiency.
It identifies five broad drivers of efficiency: markets and competition, organisation and workforce, service redesign and alternative delivery mechanisms, technology efficiencies, and estates and condition.
It separates cash releasing efficiencies from monetisable non-cash releasing efficiencies, while noting that quantifiable non-monetisable and qualitative benefits should be handled through Green Book and Magenta Book guidance.
It sets requirements for data, reporting, benefits realisation, risk management, governance, assurance, and oversight.

Inferred VfI implications:

The GEF is a strong source for the efficiency side of a VfI rubric, especially for savings validity, productivity, recurrent savings, and evidence quality.
It also helps identify subcriteria for economy, efficiency, cost-effectiveness, risk, delivery confidence, and accountability.
It should not be treated as the whole VfI method because it deliberately narrows its main scope to financial efficiency reporting.

Candidate rubric material:

Efficiency claims should show preserved or improved outputs and outcomes, not just reduced expenditure.
Strong evidence should include baselines, counterfactuals, outturns, net savings, cost-to-deliver, impacts on other bodies, maturity stage, risks, assurance, and benefits tracking.
Rubric standards can distinguish opportunities, forecast savings, delivered outputs, and realised savings using GEF maturity levels.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
Purpose and Scope
Direct source claims:
The GEF applies to bodies classified by the ONS to central government, including central government departments, agencies, most non-departmental public bodies, and arm’s-length bodies. It is expected to be adopted by officials involved in monitoring, delivery, appraisal, and evaluation of efficiency savings.
The framework aims to establish a centralised, consistent, and enduring process for departments to categorise, track, and report efficiency savings.
Inferred VfI implications:
The source is directly relevant where a VfI rubric needs to judge government efficiency claims or savings programmes. It is less complete for public value criteria that require non-monetised outcomes, distribution, rights, user experience, or legitimacy unless paired with other sources.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
Key Concepts








Concept
Direct source claim
VfI use




Efficiency
Spend less for the same or greater outputs, or produce higher outputs with the same spend.
Defines the threshold for resource-use improvement.


Productivity
Output per unit input; output or outcome must stay the same or increase to count as efficiency.
Supports standards for productivity evidence.


Technical efficiency
Achieving a given output for the lowest input.
Supports economy and operational efficiency criteria.


Allocative efficiency
Maximising outcomes by allocating resources in a wholly different way.
Supports alternative options and prevention criteria.


Drivers of efficiency
Markets, workforce, service redesign, technology, and estates.
Provides candidate subcriteria and evidence domains.


Cash releasing savings
Direct reduction in spending on an activity, project, programme, or policy.
Supports budget impact evidence.


Monetisable non-cash savings
Monetisable efficiency without a direct spending reduction.
Supports productivity, capacity, and user-time evidence.


Net efficiency
Gross efficiency less costs to deliver.
Supports cost-effectiveness and whole-cost standards.


Maturity levels
Pipeline levels 0-3 and delivery/realisation levels 4-5.
Supports performance standards and confidence ratings.


Governance and assurance
Departments should review and assure efficiency data and reporting, monitor outputs and outcomes, and provide challenge.
Supports accountability and evidence credibility criteria.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
VfI-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Cost-effectiveness and cost-benefit analysis from the Green Book are relevant when measuring technical and allocative efficiencies.
Baselines are required to measure benefits, including the current situation or starting point against which change is measured.
Cash releasing savings should be calculated from baseline cost information, a counterfactual spending profile, and outturn spending data.
Non-cash releasing savings need a comprehensive methodology with appropriate assurance.
Efficiencies should be net of costs, should not create unintended consequential costs, and should not harm performance or outcomes.
Longer-term transformation programmes should set costs and benefits out in a business case and monitor them through life.
Departments should track the efficiency measure, what drives it, where and when it will be realised, forecast savings, costs to deliver, impacts elsewhere, and actual realised savings to date.
Benefits realisation should be integrated into project, programme, and portfolio management before, during, and after delivery.
Efficiency methodology should be agreed and documented early, supported by evidence and reasonable assumptions, accurately claimed, period-aligned, reviewed, verified, and governed.
Risk management should consider delivery risk and realisation risk, especially where savings are embedded in budgets or wider portfolios.

Inferred VfI implications:
These concepts turn efficiency from a narrow savings assertion into a structured evaluative claim: there is a value proposition, a baseline, a counterfactual, an evidence standard, a delivery confidence judgement, and a check that performance and outcomes have not deteriorated.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria








Candidate rubric area
GEF material
Implication for VfI design




Economy
Markets, competition, procurement, unit cost benchmarking, estates costs.
Include subcriteria on input cost discipline and market-informed pricing.


Efficiency
Productivity, technical efficiency, service redesign, technology, workforce, demand management.
Include subcriteria on outputs per input and avoidable waste.


Cost-effectiveness
Allocative efficiency, prevention, cost-benefit and cost-effectiveness analysis, net efficiency.
Include subcriteria on alternatives and benefits relative to cost.


Effectiveness protection
No adverse impact on outcomes or strategic priorities.
Make maintained or improved outcomes a threshold condition.


Risk and resilience
Delivery risk, realisation risk, budget control risk, risk escalation.
Include evidence prompts on delivery confidence and mitigations.


Governance and accountability
Reporting, ownership, ARAC oversight, independent scrutiny, auditability.
Include subcriteria on assurance, ownership, and traceability.


Evidence quality
Quality data, baselines, counterfactuals, outturns, documentation, impartial verification.
Include standards for poor to excellent evidence strength.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
Evidence Useful for Rubric Creation

Efficiency measure description and driver.
Baseline cost, output, and outcome data.
Counterfactual spending profile and assumptions.
Forecast gross savings, costs to deliver, and net savings.
Outturn savings and realised budget or productivity impact.
Output and outcome monitoring to show performance has not fallen.
Identification of effects on other business areas, departments, local government, or users.
Maturity level from opportunity identification to realised saving.
Benefits register and benefits realisation plan.
Risk register, confidence level, mitigations, and escalation records.
Assurance records, independent scrutiny, ARAC or senior approval, and audit trail.
Evidence of avoided double counting where multiple organisations or functions are involved.

title: Government Efficiency Framework
text:
Limits and Exclusions
The GEF is primarily a financial tool for efficiency reporting. Non-monetisable benefits are outside its main scope, and the framework points users to the Green Book and Magenta Book for wider monitoring and evaluation. It should therefore inform VfI rubric components on efficiency, savings validity, and evidence quality, but it should not define the complete value judgement.
Do not treat cancellation, underspend, lower-than-forecast demand, cost deferral, reallocation to a different outcome, or income increases as efficiency evidence unless the specific GEF criteria are met.

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
This note summarises the Ministry of Defence’s Joint Service Publication 507, Investment Appraisal and Evaluation (Ministry of Defence 2014a, 2014b).
The main source page is:

GOV.UK: MOD guide to investment appraisal and evaluation (JSP 507)

The source page describes JSP 507 as MOD guidance on investment appraisal and project evaluation. It says the publication applies HM Treasury Green Book appraisal and evaluation regulations within MOD, and that JSP 507 is issued and maintained by Defence Economics. The page was first published on 1 April 2011 and last updated on 28 January 2014, when the replacement publication was split into two parts.
The two source documents are:

JSP 507 Part 1: Directive, Version 6.0, January 2014, 49 pages.
JSP 507 Part 2: Guidance, Version 6.0, January 2014, 216 pages.

Part 1 sets the directive requirements. Part 2 provides guidance and policy-compliant business practices to help users comply with the directive.

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
This note summarises the Ministry of Defence’s Joint Service Publication 507, Investment Appraisal and Evaluation (Ministry of Defence 2014a, 2014b).
The main source page is:

GOV.UK: MOD guide to investment appraisal and evaluation (JSP 507)

The source page describes JSP 507 as MOD guidance on investment appraisal and project evaluation. It says the publication applies HM Treasury Green Book appraisal and evaluation regulations within MOD, and that JSP 507 is issued and maintained by Defence Economics. The page was first published on 1 April 2011 and last updated on 28 January 2014, when the replacement publication was split into two parts.
The two source documents are:

JSP 507 Part 1: Directive, Version 6.0, January 2014, 49 pages.
JSP 507 Part 2: Guidance, Version 6.0, January 2014, 216 pages.

Part 1 sets the directive requirements. Part 2 provides guidance and policy-compliant business practices to help users comply with the directive.

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
One-Page Summary
JSP 507 is MOD’s practical handbook for applying appraisal and evaluation to defence decisions. It sits under the Green Book and translates central government appraisal principles into MOD business-case, investment-appraisal, private-sector, cost-effectiveness, risk, and evaluation practice.
Part 1 makes the policy case for appraisal and evaluation. It frames appraisal as a systematic process for clarifying the requirement, considering alternative ways of meeting it, and estimating and presenting the costs and benefits of worthwhile options. It links appraisal to MOD’s objective of demonstrating value for money and requires evaluation planning to accompany appraisals for projects, programmes, and policies that require investment appraisal.
Part 2 is the more detailed technical guide. It covers cash-flow modelling, discounting, costs and benefits, cost-effectiveness analysis, Combined Operational Effectiveness and Investment Appraisal (COEIA), infrastructure costs, personnel costs, operating costs and benefits, optimism bias, risk, uncertainty, evaluation, and private-sector involvement.
For Value for Investment (VfI), JSP 507 is not a complete rubric method. It does not provide programme-specific criteria, subcriteria, performance standards, or mixed-methods synthesis in the OPM or Verian sense. Its main contribution is different: it gives defence evaluators a disciplined evidence base for option comparison, whole-life cost, cost-effectiveness, affordability, risk, uncertainty, business-case scrutiny, and outturn evaluation.
The practical use is to combine JSP 507 with a VfI judgement architecture. VfI supplies the structure for value proposition, criteria, standards, evidence synthesis, trade-offs, and final resource-use judgement (King et al. 2023; King and Hurrell 2024). JSP 507 supplies defence-specific appraisal and evaluation evidence that can populate that structure.

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Source-to-VFI Matrix








JSP 507 material
Direct source contribution
Candidate VfI use




Appraisal principles and business cases
Establishes appraisal as structured option comparison, linked to value for money, approval, accountability, and later evaluation.
Frame the value proposition, alternatives, decision context, and baseline expectations for the VfI assessment.


Green Book relationship
Applies Green Book appraisal and evaluation regulations within MOD.
Connect defence VfI to social value, objectives, costs, benefits, risk, and unmonetised effects rather than treating defence as outside public value appraisal.


Cash-flow models and discounting
Provides guidance on time horizons, base dates, option lives, inflation, discounting, and appraisal models.
Support economy and cost-effectiveness evidence through transparent whole-life cost and time-profile assumptions.


Costs and benefits to include
Covers opportunity costs, sunk costs, common costs, hidden costs, transfer payments, double counting, and links with other budget areas.
Strengthen standards for evidence credibility and prevent weak economy or cost-effectiveness claims.


Cost-effectiveness analysis and COEIA
Recognises the role of CEA and COEIA where monetary valuation of defence benefits is limited.
Support cost-effectiveness criteria by comparing through-life cost with operational or business effectiveness rather than forcing a single benefit-cost ratio.


Infrastructure, personnel, and operating costs
Gives domain guidance for land, buildings, accommodation, equipment, leases, personnel numbers, personnel costs, redundancy, inventories, contingent liabilities, foreign exchange, revenues, and benefits.
Identify subcriteria and evidence prompts for whole-life resource stewardship across the main defence input categories.


Non-quantifiable costs and benefits
Provides guidance for recording factors that matter to value for money but cannot be quantified easily.
Feed VfI treatment of non-monetised operational, strategic, resilience, welfare, environmental, or future value.


Optimism bias, risk, and uncertainty
Covers optimism bias, risk, three-point estimating, sensitivity analysis, and contributory factors.
Define evidence standards for confidence, uncertainty, scenario testing, and risk-adjusted judgement.


Evaluation chapter and templates
Covers the purpose, focus, timing, responsibility, planning, outturn evaluation, disclosure, and template material for evaluation.
Support the VfI evidence plan, evaluation questions, learning loop, outturn comparison, and traceable judgement.


Private-sector involvement
Covers risk transfer, competition, single-option cases, outsourcing, tender evaluation, disclosure, exit strategy, and private finance.
Inform economy, efficiency, equity, and risk subcriteria where commercial strategy and supplier relationships are material to value.

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Criteria and Subcriteria Implications
The following mappings are VfI implications inferred from JSP 507. They are not a complete JSP 507 rubric and should be tailored to the programme, decision stage, and value proposition.








VfI criterion area
Candidate defence subcriteria informed by JSP 507
Evidence implications




Economy
Whole-life input stewardship; reasonable cost estimates; appropriate treatment of opportunity, sunk, common, hidden, and transfer costs; procurement and commercial discipline; infrastructure, personnel, and operating-cost completeness.
Cost models, business cases, commercial strategies, price benchmarks, cost assurance, cost-driver analysis, and audit trails should show that input choices are appropriate rather than merely cheap.


Efficiency
Option selection discipline; technical efficiency in delivery; output efficiencies; affordability management; private-sector delivery model performance; governance and scrutiny efficiency.
Evidence should show how resources are converted into outputs, whether selected options remain feasible and affordable, and whether delivery arrangements reduce avoidable delay, rework, or transaction cost.


Effectiveness
Operational or business effectiveness; achievement of intended benefits; treatment of non-quantifiable benefits; evaluation of outturn against expected results.
Evidence should include operational analysis, business-change benefits, user or beneficiary evidence, outturn evaluation, and clear explanation of benefits not captured in monetary terms.


Equity
Distribution of costs, burdens, risks, liabilities, and benefits across users, commands, staff, suppliers, taxpayers, future budgets, and other public bodies.
JSP 507 does not provide an explicit equity rubric, so VfI should use its cost, personnel, contract, risk-transfer, and liability evidence to ask who benefits, who bears cost, and whether burdens are shifted or deferred.


Cost-effectiveness
CEA and COEIA option comparison; through-life cost relative to operational or business effectiveness; alternatives and affordability; sensitivity to key assumptions.
Evidence should compare credible options, avoid unsupported monetisation, explain effectiveness measures, and test whether the preferred option remains justified under plausible scenarios.


Risk and resilience
Optimism bias, quantified and unquantified risk, uncertainty, sensitivity, risk transfer, exit strategy, contingent liabilities, and foreign exchange exposure.
Evidence standards should require risk-adjusted assumptions, transparent sensitivity analysis, and clear ownership of major delivery, financial, commercial, and operational risks.


Evidence quality and assurance
Appraisal planning, business-case checklist, scrutiny, Defence Economics advice, evaluation plans, outturn evaluation, disclosure, and lessons learned.
Higher-quality VfI evidence should be documented, reviewable, proportionate, and capable of supporting both approval and later evaluation.

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Performance Standards: Design Prompts Only
These prompts translate JSP 507 evidence discipline into VfI-style standards. They are not final standards for any specific programme.







Standard level
JSP 507-informed prompt




Poor
The appraisal or evaluation lacks credible options, omits material through-life costs, treats uncertain benefits as certain, ignores optimism bias or major risks, or cannot explain why the selected option offers value for money.


Adequate
The appraisal identifies a reasonable requirement and options, includes the main costs and benefits, records key assumptions, and has a basic evaluation plan, but evidence is incomplete or sensitivity to assumptions is only partly tested.


Good
The appraisal compares credible options using whole-life cost, cost-effectiveness or COEIA evidence where appropriate, treats risk and uncertainty explicitly, explains non-quantifiable factors, and creates an evidence trail that can support later evaluation.


Excellent
The appraisal and evaluation evidence are proportionate, transparent, risk-adjusted, independently reviewable, linked to the business case and evaluation plan, and capable of showing whether expected operational or business value was realised in practice.

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Evidence Prompts
Use these prompts when drawing on JSP 507 for a defence VfI evidence plan:

What requirement or decision is being appraised, and what resource commitment or benefit claim triggers appraisal?
What options were considered, including the status quo or minimum-change option where relevant?
What is the time horizon, base date, discounting approach, and treatment of options with different lives?
Which costs are included across acquisition, operation, support, infrastructure, personnel, training, transition, disposal, and opportunity cost?
Which costs have been excluded as sunk costs, transfer payments, common costs, or double counting, and is that treatment justified?
Are hidden costs, consequential costs, contingent liabilities, foreign exchange exposure, cancellation costs, or effects on other budgets material?
Can material benefits be monetised credibly, or should the assessment rely mainly on CEA, COEIA, break-even analysis, or rubric-based judgement?
What operational or business effectiveness measures are used, and why do they represent value in this defence context?
How are non-quantifiable costs and benefits recorded, weighted, and reported alongside quantified evidence?
What optimism bias, risk, and uncertainty assumptions are used, and how have sensitivity or scenario tests changed the judgement?
What affordability constraints affect the preferred option, and do they alter the value-for-money conclusion?
What evidence shows whether expected benefits were realised after implementation?
Who owns the evaluation plan, when will evaluation occur, and how will lessons be disseminated?
What scrutiny, assurance, Defence Economics advice, or audit trail supports the evidence?

title: JSP 507 Investment Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Limits and Exclusions
JSP 507 is a 2014 MOD appraisal and evaluation source. It predates the current Green Book, the current Magenta Book, the Government Efficiency Framework, and the Verian Value for Investment guide. It should therefore be used as a defence appraisal and evaluation evidence source, not as the whole VfI method.
Several limits matter for rubric work:

JSP 507 is appraisal and evaluation guidance, not a programme-specific criteria framework.
It is strongest on economic, financial, option, risk, and evaluation evidence.
It gives less direct help on equity, public value trade-offs, stakeholder values, and participatory standard-setting.
It supports cost-effectiveness and COEIA more directly than full cost-benefit analysis for many defence decisions.
It should be interpreted alongside Green Book and Magenta Book guidance for current central government appraisal and evaluation expectations.
VfI should provide the explicit criteria, subcriteria, standards, evidence synthesis, and overall judgement that JSP 507 does not by itself supply.

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
This note summarises the National Audit Office definition of value for money, using the NAO’s public description of its work and its VFM diagram (National Audit Office 2026, 2013).
The related source pages are:

National Audit Office: About us
National Audit Office: VFM diagram

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
This note summarises the National Audit Office definition of value for money, using the NAO’s public description of its work and its VFM diagram (National Audit Office 2026, 2013).
The related source pages are:

National Audit Office: About us
National Audit Office: VFM diagram

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
Core Idea
The NAO defines good value for money as “the optimal use of resources” to achieve intended outcomes.
This definition is grounded in public audit. The NAO reports to Parliament on whether government is delivering value for money and examines how public money has been spent. Its role is to assess delivery and resource use, not to question the policy objectives set by government.

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
The Three Es
The NAO definition is structured around economy, efficiency, and effectiveness:

Economy concerns the cost and quality of resources used as inputs.
Efficiency concerns the relationship between outputs and the resources used to produce them.
Effectiveness concerns whether intended and unintended outcomes are achieved.

The VFM diagram links resources, inputs, outputs, and outcomes. It shows that cost-effectiveness and value for money depend on the relationship between resources and outcomes, not only on whether spending was low.

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
Public Audit Framing
The NAO framing is practical and accountability-focused. It asks whether public bodies have used resources well in delivering policy objectives, whether services could be improved, and whether recommendations can help government achieve better value.
This makes the definition well suited to reviews of implementation, programme management, service delivery, procurement, and operational performance. It is less focused on selecting between options before implementation than the Green Book, but it is highly relevant to judging actual delivery.

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
Practical Implications
For value for money evaluation, the NAO definition implies that the evaluator should:

Identify the intended outcomes and the resources used.
Distinguish economy, efficiency, and effectiveness evidence.
Avoid treating savings as VfM unless quality and outcomes are protected.
Explain whether weaknesses are due to objectives, delivery, resources, governance, or external factors.
Make findings useful for accountability and improvement.

A low-cost intervention can still be poor value if it produces weak outcomes. A costly intervention can still represent good value if it delivers important outcomes efficiently and effectively relative to the resources used.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
This note summarises the second edition of OPM’s value for money guidance, Assessing Value for Money: The Oxford Policy Management Approach (King et al. 2023). The guide was written by Julian King, Daniel Wate, Esther Namukasa, Alex Hurrell, Frances Hansford, Patrick Ward, and Shiva Faramarzifar.
The main source is:

OPM PDF: Assessing Value for Money: The Oxford Policy Management Approach, second edition

Related earlier source material:

OPM: In Depth - OPM’s approach to assessing Value for Money
OPM briefing PDF: OPM’s approach to assessing Value for Money
BetterEvaluation: OPM’s approach to assessing value for money

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
One-Page Summary
OPM defines value for money as good resource use. VfM asks how well resources are used and whether that use is justified. The guide is explicit that VfM cannot be measured by a single formula, even though numeric and economic evidence can be important.
The practical contribution of the approach is a disciplined process for making evaluative judgements. It uses criteria to define the aspects of performance that matter, standards to define different levels of performance, and mixed evidence to support a transparent judgement. This makes VfM a reasoning process: evidence is organised, interpreted against agreed standards, and synthesised into a performance story.
The guidance uses the FCDO/NAO 5Es as a useful starting point: economy, efficiency, effectiveness, equity, and cost-effectiveness. These are not fixed labels to copy into every framework. Each criterion should be translated into programme-specific definitions and subcriteria. The same word, such as efficiency, can mean different things in a training programme, an adaptive governance programme, a market systems programme, or a humanitarian response.
The approach is intended to be integrated with monitoring, evaluation, and learning systems. VfM frameworks should use existing evidence where it is relevant, avoid duplicating MEL processes, and support adaptive management. The strongest VfM reports show the chain of reasoning from criteria to standards, evidence, judgement, and recommendations for improving resource use.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Concept Map






flowchart TD
  A[VfM as good resource use] --> B[Transparent evaluative judgement]
  A --> C[Opportunity cost and alternatives]
  A --> D[Learning and accountability]

  B --> E[Criteria: what matters]
  B --> F[Standards: how good is good enough]
  B --> G[Mixed evidence]
  B --> H[Performance story]

  E --> I[Economy]
  E --> J[Efficiency]
  E --> K[Effectiveness]
  E --> L[Equity]
  E --> M[Cost-effectiveness]

  I --> I1[Appropriate quality at the right price]
  J --> J1[Technical efficiency]
  J --> J2[Allocative efficiency]
  J --> J3[Dynamic efficiency]
  J --> J4[Relational efficiency]
  K --> K1[Outputs contribute to intended outcomes]
  L --> L1[Fair distribution and marginalised groups]
  M --> M1[Value created justifies resources used]

  F --> N[Rubrics: poor / adequate / good / excellent]
  G --> O[Quantitative evidence]
  G --> P[Qualitative evidence]
  G --> Q[Economic analysis]
  G --> R[MEL and programme evidence]
  G --> S[Stakeholder perspectives]

  H --> T[Traceable synthesis]
  T --> U[Judgement by criterion]
  T --> V[Overall VfM judgement]
  V --> W[Actions to improve resource use]




Figure 1: Concept map for OPM value for money guidance

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Concept Notes

VfM as Evaluative Judgement
OPM frames VfM as an evaluative question about resource use. The question is not simply whether outputs were delivered, or whether a cost ratio looks favourable. It is whether the resource use was good enough in context, given the value created, the alternatives available, and the perspectives that matter.
This is why the guide emphasises explicit evaluative reasoning. The evaluator must explain what counts as good performance before judging the evidence. That reasoning should be open to scrutiny, not hidden behind a dashboard or formula.


Opportunity Cost and Alternatives
The need for VfM assessment arises because public and donor resources have opportunity costs. Spending on one intervention means giving up other possible uses of the same resources.
In principle, good resource allocation should compare favourably with the next-best alternative. In practice, OPM recognises that alternatives and benchmarks may be unclear in complex development settings. The response is to define agreed standards for what good performance and good resource use look like in the programme context.


Criteria
Criteria are selected dimensions of programme performance that matter for good resource use. OPM discusses the 5Es: economy, efficiency, effectiveness, equity, and cost-effectiveness.
The criteria should not be treated as a generic checklist. They need to be chosen and shaped around the intervention, decision context, stakeholders, timing, and intended use of the assessment.


Standards and Rubrics
Standards define levels of performance for each criterion. OPM commonly uses rubric levels such as poor, adequate, good, and excellent.
The timing matters. Criteria and standards should be agreed before the evidence is gathered and interpreted. This gives the judgement a transparent basis and reduces the risk that evaluators retrofit the standard to the result.


Mixed Evidence
The OPM approach uses evidence from multiple sources: programme finance, operations, monitoring data, evaluation findings, qualitative inquiry, quantitative indicators, stakeholder perspectives, and economic analysis where feasible.
Economic methods can provide strong evidence, but they do not replace evaluative judgement. VfM assessment often needs to account for value that is hard to monetise, including quality, equity, sustainability, institutional change, learning, and dignity.


Traceable Synthesis
The final VfM judgement should be traceable. Readers should be able to follow the path from criteria, to programme-specific definitions, to standards, to evidence, to ratings, to the overall conclusion.
Traceability is what makes the judgement useful for accountability and learning. It also helps identify concrete opportunities to improve resource use.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
OPM Eight-Step Process
OPM presents VfM assessment as an eight-step process:

Understand the programme, including its theory of change and value proposition.
Develop VfM criteria.
Develop VfM standards.
Identify evidence requirements and select methods.
Gather evidence.
Analyse the evidence.
Synthesise findings and make judgements.
Report the judgement and opportunities for improvement.

Steps 1 to 4 design the VfM framework. Steps 5 to 8 apply it through evidence collection, analysis, synthesis, and reporting.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Creating VfM Criteria and Standards
Criteria development is the centre of the OPM approach. The aim is to define what good resource use means for the specific programme, not to copy generic definitions from another framework.
The practical sequence is:

Understand the value proposition. Clarify what the programme is trying to create, for whom, through what causal pathway, and with what resources.
Select the relevant criteria. Start with the 5Es, but include only the criteria that support useful judgement and decision making.
Write programme-specific definitions. Translate each E into the programme context.
Identify subcriteria. Choose the small number of dimensions that would represent most of the important performance story.
Define standards. Describe poor, adequate, good, and excellent performance before interpreting evidence.
Then identify evidence. OPM advises suspending measurement discussions until the criteria and standards are clear.

Useful prompts for drafting criteria:

What would make this programme’s resource use clearly good?
What would make it unacceptable, even if some indicators looked positive?
Which stakeholders need to recognise the judgement as valid?
What trade-offs are likely between cost, quality, equity, and learning?
What would be different at early, mid-point, and late stages of implementation?

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Criteria Table









Criterion
Core question
Criteria-development focus
Common evidence




Economy
Are inputs bought at the right price and quality?
Procurement, input quality, cost drivers, probity, stewardship.
Budgets, procurement records, unit costs, audit findings, market comparisons.


Efficiency
How well are inputs converted into outputs?
Technical, allocative, dynamic, and relational efficiency.
Output data, spend data, delivery timelines, portfolio decisions, adaptation logs, partner evidence.


Effectiveness
Are outputs contributing to intended outcomes?
Contribution to outcomes, quality, causality, unintended effects.
Outcome indicators, evaluation findings, contribution analysis, qualitative evidence.


Equity
Are benefits fairly distributed and are marginalised groups reached?
Access, distribution, inclusion, dignity, differential outcomes.
Disaggregated data, beneficiary research, GESI analysis, distributional analysis.


Cost-effectiveness
Does the value created justify the resources used?
Costs relative to outcomes, impacts, alternatives, and equity goals.
CEA, CBA, ROI, break-even analysis, comparator evidence, qualitative value judgement.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Economy Criteria
Economy concerns the conversion of resources into the inputs needed to deliver the intervention. The basic question is whether the programme buys inputs of appropriate quality at the right price.
OPM is clear that economy is not the same as buying the cheapest option. A low-cost input can damage VfM if it reduces quality, excludes capable suppliers, weakens delivery, creates environmental harm, or increases downstream costs.

Programme-Specific Definition Template
The programme is economical when it acts as a good steward of resources, procuring and managing inputs of appropriate quality at reasonable cost while protecting probity, delivery quality, and the wider value proposition.


Potential Subcriteria

Inputs are procured at reasonable prices relative to market conditions and required quality.
Procurement is competitive, proportionate, timely, and transparent where feasible.
Major cost drivers are understood, actively managed, and reviewed.
Financial management and probity arrangements reduce leakage, waste, and fiduciary risk.
The programme seeks economies of scale or scope where these do not weaken quality or equity.
The programme considers wider stewardship issues, such as carbon costs, local supplier development, equitable pay, and responsible sourcing.



Economy Rubric Sketch







Level
Standard




Poor
Input choices are mainly cost-driven or poorly controlled, with weak procurement, avoidable waste, or material fiduciary risk.


Adequate
Inputs are generally appropriate and major costs are controlled, but some cost drivers, procurement choices, or stewardship risks are weakly managed.


Good
The programme buys inputs of suitable quality at reasonable prices, manages major cost drivers, and can explain significant procurement choices.


Excellent
The programme demonstrates strong stewardship of financial, human, political, and environmental resources, using procurement and cost management to protect and strengthen the value proposition.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Efficiency Criteria
Efficiency concerns how well inputs are converted into outputs. FCDO’s definition aligns most closely with technical efficiency, but OPM notes that other forms of efficiency may be needed, especially for complex, adaptive, or portfolio-based programmes.
Efficiency criteria should therefore avoid over-reliance on simple cost-per-output metrics. Those metrics can be useful, but they may mislead where the quality of outputs, the choice of outputs, learning, relationships, or adaptation are central to value.

Efficiency Typology









Type
Main question
When it matters
Possible subcriteria




Technical efficiency
Are planned outputs delivered to the required quality within reasonable time and cost?
Service delivery, training, infrastructure, grants, procurement-heavy programmes.
Unit costs, timeliness, output quality, budget adherence, process productivity.


Allocative efficiency
Are resources allocated to the right activities, outputs, geographies, groups, or partners?
Portfolios, adaptive programmes, programmes with competing delivery routes.
Portfolio balance, targeting, prioritisation, stopping low-value work, shifting resources to higher-value opportunities.


Dynamic efficiency
Does the programme learn and adapt to improve resource use over time?
Innovation, governance, market systems, influencing, systems change.
Feedback loops, experimentation, learning from failure, timely adaptation, improved productivity over time.


Relational efficiency
Do relationships and coordination reduce waste and improve delivery?
Multi-stakeholder programmes, technical assistance, politically informed work, networked delivery.
Trust, coordination, partner alignment, reduced duplication, useful communication, lower transaction costs.





Programme-Specific Definition Template
The programme is efficient when it converts resources into high-quality outputs through delivery choices, portfolio decisions, adaptation, and relationships that make the best practical use of available resources.


Potential Subcriteria

Outputs are delivered to agreed quality, time, and cost expectations.
Delivery processes minimise avoidable duplication, delay, rework, and transaction costs.
Resources are allocated to the activities, groups, or workstreams most likely to create value.
The programme uses evidence to stop, adapt, or scale activities as learning emerges.
Coordination with partners improves delivery and avoids waste.
Output efficiency is not achieved at the expense of effectiveness or equity.



Efficiency Rubric Sketch







Level
Standard




Poor
Delivery is slow, costly, poorly targeted, or weakly coordinated, with limited evidence that resources are being converted into useful outputs.


Adequate
The programme delivers core outputs, but efficiency is uneven across workstreams or depends too heavily on activity completion rather than quality, targeting, or learning.


Good
The programme delivers useful outputs at reasonable cost and quality, allocates resources sensibly, and adapts delivery when evidence shows better ways to use resources.


Excellent
The programme combines strong technical, allocative, dynamic, and relational efficiency, with clear evidence that learning and coordination improve value over time.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Effectiveness Criteria
Effectiveness concerns whether outputs are having the intended effects. It moves the VfM assessment beyond delivery performance to ask whether the programme is creating the intended outcomes.
Effectiveness criteria should be grounded in the theory of change. They should also consider contribution, unintended effects, and whether outcomes are meaningful for the groups and institutions the programme is meant to support.

Programme-Specific Definition Template
The programme is effective when its outputs make a credible and meaningful contribution to intended outcomes, with evidence that the observed changes are important, contextually plausible, and consistent with the theory of change.


Potential Subcriteria

Outputs are of sufficient quality and relevance to plausibly contribute to outcomes.
Intended short- and medium-term outcomes are being achieved or are on a credible pathway.
Contribution claims are supported by evidence and do not over-claim attribution.
Important unintended positive and negative effects are identified and weighed.
The programme’s effects are meaningful to intended users, recipients, partners, or decision makers.
Effectiveness is assessed in ways that recognise context, complexity, and timing.



Effectiveness Rubric Sketch







Level
Standard




Poor
Outputs show little credible contribution to intended outcomes, or important negative effects undermine the programme’s value proposition.


Adequate
Some intended outcomes are emerging, but the contribution story is partial, uneven, or dependent on assumptions that need stronger evidence.


Good
The programme shows credible contribution to important intended outcomes, with plausible causal reasoning and attention to unintended effects.


Excellent
The programme demonstrates strong, meaningful, and well-evidenced contribution to intended outcomes, including evidence on quality, significance, and causal pathways.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Equity Criteria
Equity concerns the fair distribution of benefits and the extent to which marginalised groups are reached. OPM treats equity as central to development VfM because the purpose of many programmes is to address inequity.
Equity can also create trade-offs. Reaching the most marginalised groups may cost more per participant than reaching easier-to-serve groups. That does not automatically weaken VfM if the programme’s value proposition is fundamentally about distributive justice, rights, dignity, or inclusion.

Programme-Specific Definition Template
The programme is equitable when its design, delivery, and outcomes fairly benefit the groups it is intended to serve, especially marginalised groups, and when resource-use decisions are justified in light of distributional goals.


Potential Subcriteria

Target groups and marginalised groups are clearly defined for the programme context.
Design choices address barriers to access, participation, and benefit.
Delivery reaches marginalised groups without unacceptable exclusion, stigma, or harm.
Benefits and costs are distributed fairly across relevant groups and places.
Disaggregated evidence is used to understand who benefits, who does not, and why.
Higher costs of reaching marginalised groups are explicitly justified against the programme’s equity objectives.



Equity Rubric Sketch







Level
Standard




Poor
The programme does not adequately reach or benefit marginalised groups, or it creates avoidable exclusion, stigma, or inequitable distribution of costs and benefits.


Adequate
The programme recognises equity objectives and reaches some target groups, but evidence on access, distribution, or differential outcomes is limited or uneven.


Good
The programme makes clear, evidenced progress on equitable access and benefits, and can justify resource-use choices that support marginalised groups.


Excellent
Equity is embedded in design, delivery, evidence, and judgement, with strong evidence that marginalised groups benefit meaningfully and fairly.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Cost-Effectiveness Criteria
Cost-effectiveness asks whether the ultimate value created justifies the resources invested. OPM treats this as a companion criterion to the other Es because it looks across the results chain from inputs to outcomes or impact.
Cost-effectiveness can use economic methods such as cost-effectiveness analysis, cost-benefit analysis, return on investment, and break-even analysis. But it should not be reduced to a single ratio where important value is intangible, uncertain, long-term, or equity-focused.

Programme-Specific Definition Template
The programme is cost-effective when the value of its outcomes or impacts, including non-monetised and equity-related value, is sufficient to justify the resources used compared with realistic alternatives or expectations.


Potential Subcriteria

Outcomes or impacts and associated costs compare reasonably with relevant alternatives or benchmarks.
The costs of action are justifiable relative to the costs of doing nothing.
The value created to date, or expected future value, plausibly exceeds or justifies resources used.
Cost-effectiveness claims account for sustainability, contribution, deadweight, displacement, leakage, substitution, and externalities where relevant.
Equity-related value is included in the judgement rather than treated as a secondary consideration.



Cost-Effectiveness Rubric Sketch







Level
Standard




Poor
Costs are high relative to weak or uncertain outcomes, and the value proposition does not justify continued investment without major change.


Adequate
Outcomes provide some justification for costs, but evidence is incomplete, comparator evidence is weak, or future value depends on uncertain assumptions.


Good
Costs are justified by credible evidence of meaningful outcomes, with reasonable comparison to alternatives, expectations, or the cost of inaction.


Excellent
The programme shows strong evidence that value created or credibly expected exceeds resources used, including careful treatment of sustainability, contribution, and equity.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Practical Checklist
Use this checklist when applying the OPM approach to a programme:

Confirm the programme’s theory of change and value proposition.
Identify the main decisions the VfM assessment needs to inform.
Choose criteria that capture the most important dimensions of good resource use.
Write programme-specific definitions for each selected E.
Limit subcriteria to the dimensions that will drive meaningful judgement.
Draft poor, adequate, good, and excellent standards before gathering evidence.
Check whether each standard is specific enough to guide judgement but not so specific that it becomes an indicator list.
Identify evidence only after the rubric is clear.
Use mixed evidence: financial, operational, monitoring, evaluation, qualitative, quantitative, economic, and stakeholder evidence.
Synthesise evidence criterion by criterion before making the overall VfM judgement.
Give greater weight to criteria that matter most for the programme and timing of the assessment.
Report the reasoning clearly, including uncertainty, trade-offs, and opportunities to improve resource use.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
Examples From the Guidance

MUVA
MUVA, a female economic empowerment learning and influencing programme in Mozambique, illustrates why a simple cost-per-output view can mislead. The programme worked through experiments, learning cycles, and influencing activity, so not all value could be measured through predefined output metrics.
The VfM framework therefore treated efficiency more broadly. It considered technical efficiency, allocative efficiency across a portfolio of interventions, and dynamic efficiency in adapting, responding to feedback, and exiting interventions that were not working. Effectiveness and equity also used qualitative evidence to capture learning, influencing, and empowerment.


FSD Africa and the FSD Network
The FSD Africa example shows the approach being used to support consistency across a network while preserving flexibility for context. The framework needed to be practical, minimise reporting burden, support accountability and learning, align with theories of change, and inform investment decisions.
OPM’s response was to combine explicit criteria and standards with quantitative and qualitative evidence, link the VfM framework to existing results measurement guidance, and build on the Es approach. The point was not to force invalid comparisons across programmes, but to create a common reasoning structure.


Pakistan Sub-National Governance
The Pakistan Sub-National Governance programme shows how the approach handles complex governance reform. The programme operated across different workstreams in a changing political economy, with no straightforward external benchmarks.
The framework addressed this by co-developing standards with programme teams and DFID before evidence was gathered. It also allowed the assessment to capture emergent opportunities, learning from failure, and adaptive programming rather than judging the programme only against a fixed early workplan.

text:

See also the Verian Value for Investment Guidance note for King and Hurrell’s UK public services guide on using cost-benefit analysis as part of a broader Value for Investment assessment.
See also the OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria note for effectiveness, efficiency, impact, sustainability, relevance, and coherence.

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on bid evaluation (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Bid Evaluation Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on bid evaluation (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Bid Evaluation Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
One-Page Summary
The bid evaluation guidance explains how to design and run robust evaluation processes to identify the most economically advantageous tender. The source is clear that this is not the same as the lowest cost bid: evaluation should balance price, quality, social value, deliverability, and published award criteria.
For VfM rubric creation, the guidance is relevant because it turns procurement choice into auditable evidence. It supports rubric standards about whether evaluation criteria were objective, proportionate, relevant, clear, weighted appropriately, tested before publication, applied consistently, moderated, and documented.

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance covers assessment of bids against award criteria, not supplier selection criteria. It is aimed at practitioners designing and running bid evaluation processes.

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Bid evaluation is the process of assessing bids to identify the most economically advantageous tender.
A good evaluation is about both the final award decision and the design of a process that can identify the right supplier.
Price, quality, social value, and deliverability need to be balanced.
Quality criteria should be objective, proportionate, relevant, and clear.
Evaluation models should be tested and published, then applied as published.
Evaluation reports and bidder feedback form part of the final award process.

Inferred VfM implications:

Bid evaluation creates evidence for economy and effectiveness at the supplier-choice stage.
A rubric should test whether low price was challenged where it created deliverability risk.
Evaluation design quality is itself a VfM issue because weak criteria can select a supplier that cannot deliver value.

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Bid evaluation contribution




Economy
Tests whether price evaluation distinguishes low cost from credible value.


Effectiveness
Tests whether quality criteria are tied to service outcomes and requirements.


Equity and fairness
Tests whether criteria, scoring, moderation, and feedback are transparent and consistently applied.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether the winning bid offers the best combination of price, quality, social value, and deliverability.


Risk and resilience
Tests whether low-cost, assumption-based, or contingent pricing risks are identified.

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include bid evaluation material where it informs criteria, standards, trade-offs, evidence quality, or accountability for supplier choice.
Detailed procurement law process should be supporting context unless it affects the VfM judgement.

title: Sourcing Bid Evaluation
text:
Limits and Exclusions
This note does not cover selection-stage supplier qualification except where it affects financial standing or deliverability. Those issues are covered in the Supplier Financial Standing note.

text:

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on delivery model assessments (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Delivery Model Assessments Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on delivery model assessments (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Delivery Model Assessments Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
One-Page Summary
The delivery model assessment guidance describes an evidence-based approach for deciding whether a service, or part of a service, should be delivered in-house, procured from the market, or delivered through a hybrid model.
For VfM rubric creation, this is one of the strongest sourcing documents because it directly links alternatives, strategic fit, operational criteria, cost evidence, whole-life cost, and recommendation-making. It supports a rubric standard that a procurement is not good VfM merely because the selected supplier is competitive; the selected delivery model must also be justified against feasible alternatives.

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance supports departments in creating and running an analytical process for delivery model choice. It states that delivery model assessment is not a target operating model, budget mechanism, full sourcing strategy, or supplier evaluation. It is a structured decision process for choosing the mechanism for service delivery.

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Delivery model assessment is an analytical, evidence-based approach to support a recommendation on in-house, market, or hybrid delivery.
The process should define the service and delivery options, identify strategic and operational evaluation criteria, build or use a should-cost model, conduct evaluation, align cost and non-cost evidence, and document recommendations.
Strategic and operational criteria can include strategy, policy, service characteristics, people and assets, market and suppliers, risk, controls, and transition considerations.
Cost evaluation should consider whole-life costs rather than only immediate procurement cost.

Inferred VfM implications:

Delivery model assessment supplies the alternatives logic for cost-effectiveness.
It provides candidate subcriteria for strategic fit, operational deliverability, market viability, capability, transition risk, and resilience.
It helps avoid retrofitting a procurement justification after the preferred route has already been chosen.

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Delivery model assessment contribution




Economy
Tests whether cost evidence covers whole-life resource use and realistic delivery options.


Efficiency
Tests whether the delivery model avoids avoidable transaction cost, duplication, transition waste, or management burden.


Effectiveness
Tests whether the model is capable of delivering the required service quality and outcomes.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether the recommended model is justified against in-house, outsourced, hybrid, public sector, third sector, and other feasible options.


Risk and resilience
Tests whether risks differ by delivery model and whether the preferred model can manage them.


Capability
Tests whether the authority or market has the people, assets, data, and management capability needed.

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include delivery model content where it informs alternatives, criteria, standards, evidence, and trade-offs. It is directly relevant to rubric creation because it defines the decision frame before procurement or service redesign.

title: Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment
text:
Limits and Exclusions
Detailed operating model design is out of scope unless it changes the evidence used to judge VfM. Supplier-specific evaluation belongs in the Bid Evaluation note rather than this note.

text:

title: Sourcing Market Management
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on market management (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Market Management Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Market Management
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on market management (Cabinet Office 2021).
The main source is:

GOV.UK PDF: Market Management Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Market Management
text:
One-Page Summary
The market management guidance defines market management as understanding the markets government sources from, recognising government’s influence on those markets, and designing commercial strategies and contracts that promote healthy markets in the short, medium, and long term.
For VfM rubric creation, the guidance is directly relevant to economy, resilience, supplier access, competition, and long-term value. It treats market health as part of good commercial stewardship, not as a background condition.

title: Sourcing Market Management
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance applies to in-scope outsourcing procurements and encourages wider public sector use. It introduces rules and guidance for market health assessment and for adapting commercial strategy and contract design to address market weaknesses.

title: Sourcing Market Management
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Better market management should lead to more competitive markets, less over-reliance on one or two suppliers, and improved value for money.
Departments should conduct market health assessments and consider how commercial strategy and contract design can respond to market weaknesses.
Strategic outline business cases should demonstrate appropriate market assessment and consideration of options to promote market health.
Indicators can cover market structure, contestability, market outcomes, supplier concentration, switching, and market trends.

Inferred VfM implications:

Economy depends on sustainable competition, not just the lowest price in a single tender.
Risk and resilience should test dependence on limited suppliers or fragile markets.
Equity and fairness can include whether procurement design supports supplier access and avoids unnecessary barriers.

title: Sourcing Market Management
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Market management contribution




Economy
Tests whether price and competition are supported by a healthy, contestable market.


Efficiency
Tests whether market engagement reduces avoidable sourcing failure, rework, or delay.


Equity and fairness
Tests whether market design supports transparent access for capable suppliers, including smaller providers where relevant.


Risk and resilience
Tests over-reliance, switching difficulty, supplier fragility, and market concentration.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether the commercial strategy preserves long-term value rather than short-term price only.

title: Sourcing Market Management
Sourcing Market Management
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include market material where it informs competition, market stewardship, supplier access, resilience, alternatives, or long-term value.
Exclude generic market description unless it affects a criterion, standard, evidence need, or trade-off.

title: Sourcing Market Management
text:
Limits and Exclusions
Market health is not sufficient evidence of VfM by itself. It should be combined with delivery model, cost, quality, risk, and benefits evidence.

text:

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on risk allocation and pricing approaches (Cabinet Office 2026).
The main source is:

GOV.UK HTML: Risk Allocation and Pricing Approaches Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on risk allocation and pricing approaches (Cabinet Office 2026).
The main source is:

GOV.UK HTML: Risk Allocation and Pricing Approaches Guidance Note

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
One-Page Summary
The risk allocation and pricing guidance explains that inappropriate or disproportionate risk allocation is widely recognised as a reason government contracts underperform or fail. It emphasises that the aim is not to transfer as much risk as possible to suppliers, but to allocate risk appropriately between parties and reflect that allocation in pricing and payment mechanisms.
For VfM rubric creation, this is a key source for risk, resilience, pricing, incentives, and sustainability. It supports a standard that a contract may be poor VfM if risk transfer produces underperformance, excessive risk pricing, supplier distress, or incentives outside the supplier’s control.

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance supports contracting authorities when considering risk allocation as part of commercial strategy. It covers risk identification, analysis, evaluation, treatment, allocation, pricing structures, payment mechanisms, incentivisation, disincentivisation, and inflation management.

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Effective risk management is crucial to successful contract delivery.
Inappropriate risk balance can lead to poor value for money and underperformance.
Risk should be allocated to the party best able to manage it, bearing in mind ability to assess, price, reduce, mitigate, and respond to the risk.
Transferring risk can promote VfM where the supplier adds value in bearing and managing that risk.
Payment mechanisms and pricing approaches should align incentives with contracted requirements and realistic risk ownership.

Inferred VfM implications:

Risk allocation should be a rubric criterion or subcriterion for complex services.
Pricing evidence should be interpreted with risk allocation, not separately from it.
Rubric standards should test whether incentives are within the supplier’s control and whether government retained risks it is better placed to manage.

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Risk allocation and pricing contribution




Economy
Tests whether risk pricing is efficient rather than inflated by inappropriate transfer.


Efficiency
Tests whether payment mechanisms incentivise timely, productive delivery.


Effectiveness
Tests whether incentives support the outcomes and performance standards required.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether the risk-price trade-off improves overall value relative to alternatives.


Risk and resilience
Tests whether risks are identified, allocated, monitored, and managed through contract life.

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include risk and pricing material where it informs trade-offs, incentives, affordability, deliverability, supplier behaviour, or resilience.
Exclude generic descriptions of pricing models unless they affect the VfM standard for the contract being judged.

title: Sourcing Risk Allocation and Pricing
text:
Limits and Exclusions
This note does not specify the correct pricing model for every contract. Rubric standards should be tailored to the service, risk profile, supplier market, and delivery model.

text:

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on assessing and monitoring the economic and financial standing of bidders and suppliers (Cabinet Office 2026).
The main source is:

GOV.UK HTML: Assessing and Monitoring the Economic and Financial Standing of Bidders and Suppliers

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on assessing and monitoring the economic and financial standing of bidders and suppliers (Cabinet Office 2026).
The main source is:

GOV.UK HTML: Assessing and Monitoring the Economic and Financial Standing of Bidders and Suppliers

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
One-Page Summary
The guidance explains how to assess bidders’ financial capacity before award, monitor suppliers’ financial standing during contract life, and mitigate identified financial risks. Its purpose is to safeguard public service delivery.
For VfM rubric creation, the source is relevant because a supplier that cannot financially perform a contract creates delivery, continuity, cost, and service failure risks. It also warns that assessment must be proportionate and fair: financial standing should be interpreted through contract criticality, risk mitigations, and supplier type rather than used mechanically to exclude suppliers.

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
Purpose and Scope
The guidance covers pre-award assessment, risk mitigation, contractual provisions, ongoing monitoring, key suppliers, liquidity, forward-looking information, annual confirmation, financial distress events, standard financial ratios, and information sources.

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
VfM-Relevant Concepts
Direct source claims:

Assessing and monitoring economic and financial standing is about understanding suppliers’ capacity to perform a contract.
The assessment should support both pre-award decision-making and post-award monitoring.
Conditions must be proportionate to ensuring that suppliers have the financial capacity to perform the contract.
All bidders should be treated consistently unless relevant differences justify different treatment.
Contract criticality should inform the level of assessment and mitigation required.
Financial ratios and risk tools can inform assessment but should not be used mechanically without judgement.

Inferred VfM implications:

Supplier financial standing is evidence for risk and resilience, not simply a pass/fail compliance step.
A VfM rubric should test proportionality: excessive financial thresholds may harm competition, while weak monitoring may expose public services to failure.
Monitoring after award is part of good resource use where contracts are critical or suppliers are financially material.

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
Implications for Rubric Criteria







VfM criterion
Supplier financial standing contribution




Economy
Tests whether financial requirements support competition without unnecessary exclusion.


Effectiveness
Tests whether the supplier can sustain service delivery.


Equity and fairness
Tests whether financial standing checks treat suppliers consistently and proportionately.


Risk and resilience
Tests contract criticality, supplier liquidity, distress indicators, mitigations, and continuity planning.


Cost-effectiveness
Tests whether financial risk mitigation is proportionate to contract value and service criticality.

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
Relevance Rules Applied
Include supplier financial standing material where it informs delivery risk, continuity, proportionality, competition, mitigations, or post-award monitoring.
Exclude detailed ratio formulae unless a specific rubric needs to assess the credibility of financial analysis.

title: Sourcing Supplier Financial Standing
text:
Limits and Exclusions
Financial standing evidence does not by itself prove VfM. It should be interpreted alongside market health, bid quality, pricing, delivery model, and contract performance evidence.

text:

title: Verian Value for Investment Guidance
text:
This note summarises King and Hurrell’s Verian guide, A Guide to Evaluation of Value for Money in UK Public Services: Why Cost-Benefit Analysis Alone May Be Insufficient to Evaluate VfM, and How to Navigate a Solution (King and Hurrell 2024).
The main sources are:

Verian guide page: A guide to evaluation of Value for Money
Verian PDF: A Guide to Evaluation of Value for Money in UK Public Services
Julian King mirror: Verian Group Value for Money Guide, August 2024

title: Verian Value for Investment Guidance
text:
One-Page Summary
King and Hurrell argue that value for money evaluation should not be reduced to cost-benefit analysis. CBA can be valuable evidence where costs and benefits can be credibly quantified and monetised, but many public services create value that is broader, contested, long term, uncertain, or difficult to monetise.
The guide introduces Value for Investment as an interdisciplinary approach to VfM. It combines economics and evaluation by using mixed methods, explicit criteria, agreed standards, and evaluative reasoning. The aim is not to avoid economic analysis. The aim is to use economic analysis where it is appropriate, while also recognising social, environmental, cultural, institutional, distributional, and other forms of public value.
The practical implication is that evaluators should design the VfM assessment around the decision context and value proposition. They should identify what value matters, who it matters to, what resources have been invested, which effects can be monetised, which effects require other evidence, and how trade-offs will be judged transparently.
