title: Verian Value for Investment Guidance
text:
Concept Notes

VfM Is Broader Than CBA
The guide’s central distinction is between VfM as the evaluation question and CBA as one possible method. A CBA can estimate whether monetised benefits exceed monetised costs, but VfM assessment may need to weigh several criteria at different stages of a policy or programme.
This matters because public service interventions often produce value that is hard to capture in a single monetised ratio. Distribution, dignity, trust, resilience, prevention, user experience, environmental effects, institutional capability, and cultural value may be material even when they are not easily priced.


CBA as Evidence, Not the Whole Judgement
King and Hurrell do not reject CBA. They treat it as a potentially important input where monetisation is feasible, proportionate, and credible. The problem arises when CBA is treated as synonymous with VfM or when the monetised result crowds out important unmonetised evidence.
In a Value for Investment assessment, CBA can sit alongside qualitative evidence, quantitative indicators, stakeholder evidence, implementation evidence, and rubric-based assessment. The final judgement should explain what the CBA covers, what it omits, and how omitted value affects the overall conclusion.


Multiple Values
The guide broadens the value proposition beyond narrowly financial or monetised benefits. Evaluators should ask what kinds of value the investment is intended to create, for whom, and through what pathway.
The values may include economic value, social value, environmental value, cultural value, public service quality, equity, capability, risk reduction, learning, and future optionality. The relevant set should be chosen for the policy or programme rather than copied mechanically from a generic list.


Mixed Evidence
Value for Investment uses mixed methods. Quantitative data can describe costs, outputs, outcomes, reach, timing, and trends. Qualitative evidence can explain how and why value was created, whose value counts, what trade-offs emerged, and what contextual factors shaped performance.
Economic analysis remains part of this evidence mix. The key design choice is whether it should be the main method, a supporting line of evidence, or infeasible for the specific question and data conditions.


Explicit Criteria and Standards
The approach uses evaluative reasoning: evidence is interpreted against criteria and standards. Criteria define the dimensions of value and resource use that matter. Standards define what would count as poor, adequate, good, or excellent performance before the evidence is judged.
This makes the judgement more transparent. Readers can see how evidence has been weighed, where evidence is weak, which trade-offs matter, and why the overall conclusion follows from the criteria.

title: Verian Value for Investment Guidance
text:
Relationship to Cost-Benefit Analysis









Approach
Main contribution
Main limitation
Best use




CBA-led evaluation
Produces a monetised comparison of costs and benefits, often through net present value or benefit-cost ratio.
Can understate value when important effects cannot be credibly monetised or when distribution and non-market effects matter.
Use when the main costs and benefits can be quantified and monetised with enough confidence to support decision making.


Rubric/evaluative judgement VfM
Interprets mixed evidence against explicit criteria and standards.
Does not produce a single comparable monetary metric and depends on the quality of criteria, standards, and evidence synthesis.
Use when value is multidimensional, evidence is mixed, or judgement needs to include non-monetised effects.


Combined Value for Investment assessment
Uses CBA where useful while integrating wider evidence and criteria into a traceable VfM judgement.
Requires careful design so the economic evidence and evaluative criteria are aligned rather than competing.
Use when monetised analysis is informative but incomplete, or when decision makers need a broader account of public value.

title: Verian Value for Investment Guidance
text:
Value for Investment Process
The guide presents Value for Investment as a practical design and implementation process.

Design Phase

Understand the programme, policy, investment, decision context, and theory of change.
Define the value proposition: what value is expected, for whom, and through which resources and activities.
Develop evaluation questions that distinguish the overall VfM question from subquestions about economy, efficiency, effectiveness, equity, cost-effectiveness, or other relevant criteria.
Select criteria that reflect the important dimensions of public value and good resource use.
Define standards so that the assessment has a transparent basis for judgement.
Identify evidence needs and methods, including whether CBA, CEA, break-even analysis, rubric assessment, qualitative inquiry, or other methods are appropriate.



Implementation Phase

Gather evidence from cost data, monitoring data, outcome evidence, stakeholder perspectives, economic analysis, implementation evidence, and contextual analysis.
Analyse evidence against each criterion and standard.
Synthesise the evidence into criterion-level judgements.
Make an overall VfM judgement that explains trade-offs, uncertainty, omitted evidence, and the relative contribution of monetised and non-monetised value.
Report findings in a way that supports accountability, learning, and future resource allocation.

title: Verian Value for Investment Guidance
text:
Practical Note-Taking Prompts
Use these prompts when applying the guide to a programme or policy:







Prompt
Notes to capture




What value is being assessed?
Intended outcomes, public value, service quality, risk reduction, capability, learning, prevention, equity, or other material value.


For whom?
Users, citizens, taxpayers, public bodies, delivery partners, places, future beneficiaries, and groups affected differently.


What resources are used?
Public spending, staff time, assets, political capital, data, partner resources, opportunity costs, and environmental resources.


Which values can and cannot be monetised?
Effects suitable for CBA, effects better handled through quantitative non-monetised evidence, and effects needing qualitative judgement.


Which criteria and standards will guide judgement?
Programme-specific criteria, subcriteria, and performance standards agreed before evidence is interpreted.


What evidence is needed?
Economic, financial, monitoring, outcome, process, qualitative, stakeholder, comparative, and contextual evidence.


How will trade-offs be reported?
Tensions between cost, quality, equity, speed, risk, uncertainty, monetised value, and wider public value.

title: Verian Value for Investment Guidance
text:
Practical Implications
For UK public service evaluation, the Verian guide supports a method choice sequence:

Start with the VfM question and decision context, not with a preferred method.
Decide whether CBA can credibly answer all or part of the question.
Identify the material non-monetised values that still need to be judged.
Build criteria and standards that make those judgements explicit.
Use mixed evidence to assess performance criterion by criterion.
Explain how monetised and non-monetised evidence combine in the final judgement.

The strongest VfM notes therefore show both the economic evidence and the evaluative reasoning. They do not hide behind a single ratio, but they also do not ignore quantitative or economic evidence where it can strengthen the assessment.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Core Idea
Authoritative definitions agree that value for money is a judgement about resources and results, not a single financial metric. The sources differ in emphasis:

HM Treasury frames VfM as a balanced appraisal judgement about public resources, objectives, social value, monetisable and unmonetisable effects, distribution, and risk.
The NAO frames VfM as the optimal use of resources through economy, efficiency, and effectiveness to achieve intended outcomes.
The OECD DAC criteria frame evaluation as a judgement of merit or worth, with efficiency asking whether resources are justified by results.

Together, these definitions support a broad but disciplined view of VfM. A credible VfM note should explain what objectives matter, what resources were used, what results were achieved, what effects were not monetised, and how trade-offs were judged.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Comparison of Definitions









Source
Short definition
Main emphasis
Practical implication




Green Book
“balanced judgement about the optimal use of public resources”
Social value, objectives, monetised and unmonetised effects, distribution, risk
Do not rank options by benefit-cost ratio alone. Show the full judgement.


Green Book supplementary guidance
“finding the best way to use public resources”
Applying the Green Book method and five case model
Tie VfM to objectives, strategic fit, economic appraisal, and decision advice.


NAO
“optimal use of resources” to achieve intended outcomes
Economy, efficiency, effectiveness
Test whether spending was used well in delivering policy objectives.


OECD DAC
Criteria are “not a methodology or approach”
Evaluation questions and lenses for judging interventions
Use criteria thoughtfully; do not treat them as a mechanical checklist.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
What the Definitions Share
All three institutional framings require explicit objectives. An option or intervention cannot be good value if it does not achieve the purpose it was meant to serve.
They also require more than cost minimisation. The NAO’s economy criterion asks whether inputs were obtained at the right cost and quality, but its definition also includes efficiency and effectiveness. HM Treasury is even more explicit that monetisable costs and benefits are only part of the judgement. OECD similarly treats efficiency as one lens among relevance, coherence, effectiveness, impact, and sustainability.
The common pattern is:

Define the objectives and decision context.
Identify the resources used or proposed.
Assess results, benefits, costs, quality, risk, and distribution.
Explain the judgement, including trade-offs and uncertainty.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Differences in Emphasis
The Green Book is strongest for appraisal. It is designed to help decision makers choose between options before implementation, using social cost-benefit analysis or social cost-effectiveness analysis where appropriate.
The NAO definition is strongest for public audit. It looks at whether government has used money well in delivering policy objectives, while not questioning the policy objectives themselves.
The OECD DAC criteria are strongest for evaluation design and synthesis. They broaden the judgement beyond resource conversion alone, especially where relevance, coherence, impact, sustainability, or equity concerns affect whether an intervention is worth continuing or scaling.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Practical Implications
For value for money evaluation, these definitions imply that the evaluator should:

State the VfM definition being used.
Link the definition to the decision or accountability question.
Avoid treating a benefit-cost ratio as the whole VfM answer.
Include non-monetised benefits, costs, risks, and distributional effects where they matter.
Use criteria or rubrics to make the final judgement traceable.

The most useful definition is therefore not just a sentence. It is a working framework for deciding what evidence must be collected and how mixed evidence will be interpreted.

title: Defence VfI Rubric Generation
Defence VfI Rubric Generation
Defence VfI Rubric Generation
text:
Source Roles
Each source should play a specific role in rubric generation. Do not copy every source heading into the rubric. Use the sources to define what good resource use means in the programme context.








Source
Use in rubric generation
Avoid




Verian Value for Investment
Define the value proposition, mixed evidence needs, criteria, standards, trade-offs, and final VfI judgement.
Treating cost-benefit analysis as the whole VfM answer.


OPM VfM guidance
Structure the design sequence from criteria to standards to evidence and synthesis.
Copying generic 5E definitions without programme-specific interpretation.


Green Book
Anchor objectives, public value, options, social costs and benefits, distribution, risk, uncertainty, and decision advice.
Assuming the highest benefit-cost ratio automatically determines the VfI judgement.


JSP 507
Translate appraisal and evaluation evidence into defence terms: requirement, options, through-life cost, CEA, COEIA, risk, affordability, and outturn evaluation.
Forcing monetised CBA where defence benefits are operational, strategic, uncertain, or hard to monetise.


Sourcing Playbook
Test commercial preparation, delivery model choice, market evidence, should-cost evidence, bid evaluation, risk allocation, benefits, and supplier resilience.
Turning procedural sourcing steps into criteria when they do not affect value judgement.


Government Efficiency Framework
Strengthen efficiency and savings standards: baselines, counterfactuals, net savings, recurrence, double counting, assurance, and outcome protection.
Treating financial efficiency reporting as a complete public value assessment.



The core rule is source discipline. Verian and OPM define the judgement architecture. Green Book, JSP 507, sourcing guidance, and GEF provide source-specific prompts, evidence tests, and standards for particular parts of that architecture.

title: Defence VfI Rubric Generation
text:
Generation Workflow

1. Define the Decision Context and Value Proposition
Start by defining the decision the evaluation must inform. A rubric for an early approval decision will not look the same as a rubric for an in-flight programme review, contract re-let, benefits evaluation, or post-project outturn assessment.
Record:

the programme boundary, lifecycle stage, and decision point;
the operational, organisational, commercial, or estate problem being addressed;
the main alternatives, including do minimum, upgrade, outsourcing, insourcing, framework route, allied collaboration, or non-materiel options;
the intended value for users, commands, maintainers, suppliers, allies, taxpayers, communities, and future programmes;
the resources consumed, including acquisition, support, workforce, facilities, training, data, opportunity cost, and through-life funding;
the conditions that must hold for value to emerge, such as user adoption, integration, threat assumptions, supportability, supplier performance, and affordability.

This becomes the value proposition: what value is expected, for whom, through which resources and mechanisms, compared with which alternatives.


2. Identify Source-Relevant Value Dimensions
Read the value proposition against the source set. The aim is to identify material value dimensions, not to produce a long source checklist.







Source lens
Questions to ask




Green Book
What objectives and critical success factors define success? What social value, distribution, unmonetised effects, risks, and uncertainties are material?


JSP 507
What requirement, options, through-life costs, effectiveness measures, affordability constraints, and defence risks need appraisal or evaluation evidence?


Sourcing
What delivery model, market, supplier, pricing, payment, KPI, risk allocation, and benefits evidence affects value?


GEF
Are any efficiency or savings claims being made? Are they technical or allocative, cash releasing or non-cash releasing, forecast or realised?


Verian and OPM
Which value dimensions require explicit criteria and standards because evidence will be mixed, contested, uncertain, or non-monetised?



At this stage, keep a source traceability log. For each candidate value dimension, note which source justifies considering it and whether the source claim is direct or an inference.


3. Select Criteria
Select a small set of criteria that will answer the VfI question. The default defence starting point is economy, efficiency, effectiveness, equity or distribution, and cost-effectiveness. Add risk and resilience, learning and capability, or evidence quality only where they materially affect the judgement and cannot be handled as subcriteria.
Good criteria should:

reflect the programme’s value proposition;
cover the main resource-to-value pathway;
be distinct enough to avoid double counting;
support standards and evidence collection;
make the main trade-offs visible to decision makers.

Avoid criteria that are only data headings, process steps, or source chapter titles. A criterion should name something that can be judged.


4. Derive Subcriteria
Subcriteria translate each criterion into the dimensions of performance that matter for the programme. They should be few enough to support judgement and specific enough to guide evidence collection.
Use source prompts selectively:

Green Book prompts help derive subcriteria on objectives, alternatives, social value, distribution, risk, uncertainty, and unmonetised effects.
JSP 507 prompts help derive subcriteria on requirement clarity, whole-life cost, CEA or COEIA, affordability, optimism bias, risk, and outturn evaluation.
Sourcing prompts help derive subcriteria on market health, delivery model, should-cost, quality, risk allocation, incentives, benefits, supplier resilience, and framework use.
GEF prompts help derive subcriteria on productivity, technical efficiency, allocative efficiency, net savings, recurrence, no adverse outcome impact, and assurance.
OPM and Verian prompts help test whether the subcriteria support mixed-evidence synthesis and transparent judgement.

For each subcriterion, write a one-sentence judgement question. If a subcriterion cannot be turned into a judgement question, it is probably not ready.


5. Write Performance Standards
Write standards before interpreting evidence. Use four levels unless the evaluation has a reason to use a different scale:







Level
Meaning




Poor
Minimum expectations are not met, or weaknesses make resource use hard to justify.


Adequate
Minimum expectations are met, but important weaknesses, gaps, or risks remain.


Good
Performance meets reasonable expectations, evidence supports a positive judgement, and material risks are controlled.


Excellent
Performance exceeds reasonable expectations, trade-offs are explicit, evidence is strong, and learning or assurance supports confidence.



Standards should be specific to the criterion. For example, “good efficiency” in a digital programme may require evidence of adaptation, reuse, and reduced manual effort; “good efficiency” in an equipment programme may require schedule realism, integration flow, and avoidable rework control.
Do not write standards as numeric thresholds alone. Quantitative thresholds can be useful, but the standard should also state how quality, risk, distribution, and evidence confidence affect the judgement.


6. Specify Evidence and Methods
Evidence should follow the criteria and standards. Do not start with available data and retrofit the rubric around it.
Common evidence types include:

business cases, approvals, options appraisals, and Green Book or JSP 507 analysis;
whole-life cost models, should-cost models, budgets, forecasts, outturns, and cost assurance;
COEIA, cost-effectiveness analysis, break-even analysis, sensitivity analysis, and scenario testing;
commercial strategies, delivery model assessments, market engagement, bid evaluation, contracts, KPIs, and supplier evidence;
GEF efficiency baselines, counterfactuals, net savings calculations, benefits tracking, and assurance records;
operational analysis, trials, user research, acceptance evidence, service data, readiness, reliability, and support evidence;
interviews, workshops, surveys, document review, contribution analysis, process evaluation, and stakeholder evidence.

For each evidence item, specify what criterion it informs, what standard it helps judge, what quality checks are needed, and what limitations should be reported.


7. Check Consistency, Gaps, Trade-Offs, and Assurance
Before evidence collection starts, review the draft rubric.
Ask:

Does every criterion trace back to the value proposition and at least one source rationale?
Are criteria distinct, or do they double count the same issue?
Are the standards clear enough that two evaluators could apply them consistently?
Does the evidence plan include both monetised and non-monetised value where material?
Are alternatives, opportunity cost, and affordability visible?
Are distribution, risk, uncertainty, and future liabilities visible?
Are efficiency claims tested against baselines, counterfactuals, netting, recurrence, double counting, and outcome protection?
Is there enough evidence to judge commercial value, delivery model choice, supplier resilience, and benefits realisation where sourcing is material?
What gaps will remain, and how will they affect confidence in the final judgement?

The output of this step should be a rubric that can be used for evidence collection, analysis, synthesis, and reporting without redesigning the framework halfway through the evaluation.

title: Defence VfI Rubric Generation
text:
Reusable Criteria Bank
Use this bank as a starting point, then tailor it. Not every criterion belongs in every rubric.









Criterion
Defence definition
Candidate subcriteria
Source anchors




Economy
Stewardship of financial, workforce, supplier, estate, data, and specialist inputs at appropriate cost and quality.
Price reasonableness; input quality; whole-life cost control; procurement discipline; scarce skills; false economy risks.
OPM, Green Book, JSP 507, Sourcing Playbook.


Efficiency
Conversion of resources into useful outputs through delivery, allocation, learning, and coordination.
Technical efficiency; allocative efficiency; dynamic efficiency; relational efficiency; schedule and rework control; governance speed.
OPM, GEF, JSP 507, Sourcing Playbook.


Effectiveness
Delivery of intended operational, organisational, user, service, or estate outcomes.
Capability delivered; outcome contribution; reliability and availability; user adoption; interoperability; unintended effects.
Verian, Green Book, JSP 507.


Equity and distribution
Fair distribution of benefits, burdens, access, risks, and future liabilities.
User and maintainer burden; cross-force access; place and community effects; supplier access; future budget pressure; intergenerational effects.
Green Book, OPM, Verian, sourcing guidance.


Cost-effectiveness
Whether value created justifies the resources used compared with credible alternatives.
Through-life cost relative to outcomes; options comparison; COEIA or CEA; unmonetised value; sensitivity; opportunity cost.
Green Book, JSP 507, Verian, OPM.


Risk and resilience
Whether major delivery, operational, commercial, financial, cyber, safety, and continuity risks are understood and managed.
Optimism bias; risk ownership; contingency; supplier resilience; supportability; exit routes; scenario robustness.
JSP 507, Green Book, Sourcing Playbook.


Learning and capability
Whether the programme improves future resource use, institutional capability, and adaptive performance.
Lessons applied; knowledge transfer; data quality; reusable assets; capability development; reduced future dependency.
OPM, Verian, sourcing and consultancy guidance.


Evidence quality and assurance
Whether the judgement is based on proportionate, transparent, reviewable, and decision-relevant evidence.
Source traceability; data quality; independent assurance; sensitivity testing; audit trail; limitations.
OPM, Verian, Green Book, JSP 507, GEF.

title: Defence VfI Rubric Generation
text:
Rubric Templates

Criteria Definition Template







Field
Drafting prompt




Criterion name
Use a short judgement term such as economy, effectiveness, or risk and resilience.


Programme-specific definition
Describe what good performance means for this programme, not in generic terms.


Main judgement question
Ask the question the criterion must answer.


Source rationale
Cite the source prompts that justify including the criterion.


Boundaries
State what is inside and outside the criterion to avoid double counting.


Main trade-offs
Identify tensions with cost, quality, speed, risk, equity, or future value.





Subcriteria Template










Criterion
Subcriterion
Judgement question
Candidate evidence
Source trace




Economy
Whole-life cost realism
Are acquisition, support, personnel, infrastructure, training, transition, disposal, and opportunity costs complete enough for judgement?
Cost model, JSP 507 appraisal, should-cost model, business case, cost assurance.
JSP 507, Green Book, sourcing.


Efficiency
Valid efficiency claim
Does the claimed saving increase output for the same input, maintain output for lower input, or reallocate resources to a better route without adverse outcome impact?
Baseline, counterfactual, outturn, benefits log, net savings calculation, outcome indicators.
GEF, OPM.


Effectiveness
Operational value
Does the capability or service perform in the conditions that matter to users and commanders?
Trials, acceptance evidence, operational analysis, readiness data, user evidence.
JSP 507, Verian.


Risk and resilience
Supplier continuity
Is supplier financial standing, delivery resilience, and exit planning proportionate to the criticality of the service?
Financial standing checks, continuity plan, contract risk register, market assessment.
Sourcing Playbook.





Performance Standards Template










Criterion
Poor
Adequate
Good
Excellent




Economy
Material input costs, quality thresholds, or cost drivers are not understood; stewardship weaknesses create avoidable waste or false economy.
Main costs and inputs are identified, but some assumptions, quality risks, or cost drivers are weakly evidenced.
Inputs are appropriate quality at reasonable cost, with whole-life cost and major cost drivers actively managed.
Stewardship is strong across financial, workforce, supplier, estate, and data inputs; choices protect long-term value and are independently reviewable.


Efficiency
Delivery or savings claims rely on weak baselines, cost shifting, deferral, reduced output, or avoidable delay and rework.
Delivery is plausible and basic baselines exist, but output quality, net savings, recurrence, or adaptation evidence is incomplete.
Resources are converted into useful outputs with controlled delay and rework; efficiency claims are net, evidenced, and outcome-protecting.
Technical, allocative, dynamic, and relational efficiency are strong; realised efficiencies are sustained, assured, and used to improve future resource use.


Effectiveness
Outputs do not meet minimum operational, service, or user requirements, or the contribution to intended outcomes is not credible.
Core outputs are delivered but evidence of quality, uptake, contribution, or unintended effects is partial.
Outputs meet the conditions that matter, users can exploit them, and evidence supports a credible outcome contribution.
Operational or service value is strong across demanding conditions, with clear contribution evidence, managed unintended effects, and learning for future decisions.


Cost-effectiveness
The programme cannot show credible alternatives, complete enough costs, or a defensible relationship between resources and value.
The programme compares some options and costs, but important unmonetised value, uncertainty, or opportunity cost is weakly treated.
Whole-life cost and effectiveness evidence show that the programme compares well with credible alternatives, including non-monetised value and uncertainty.
The judgement is risk-adjusted, transparent, and robust across plausible scenarios; decision makers can see why the resource use is justified overall.





Evidence Plan Template











Evidence item
Criterion informed
Method or source
Quality checks
Likely limitation
Use in synthesis




Business case and options appraisal
Cost-effectiveness, risk, evidence quality
Document review
Check option set, assumptions, appraisal boundary, sensitivity, and treatment of unmonetised effects.
May reflect forecast rather than realised performance.
Establish baseline expectations and alternatives.


Whole-life cost and should-cost models
Economy, cost-effectiveness
Cost review
Check completeness, base date, inflation, optimism bias, hidden costs, common costs, and assurance.
May not capture future operating changes or classified dependencies.
Test cost realism and affordability.


Trials, acceptance, and user evidence
Effectiveness, risk
Mixed methods
Check relevance to operational conditions, user groups, and limitations.
Trial conditions may differ from deployed context.
Judge whether outputs plausibly create intended value.


Efficiency benefits records
Efficiency, evidence quality
GEF-style benefits review
Check baseline, counterfactual, gross/net treatment, recurrence, double counting, and outcome protection.
Forecast savings may not yet be realised.
Distinguish claimed, forecast, and realised efficiency.


Commercial and supplier evidence
Economy, risk, resilience
Commercial document review and interviews
Check delivery model rationale, market evidence, risk allocation, payment mechanism, and supplier standing.
Supplier data may be commercially sensitive or incomplete.
Judge whether commercial choices support sustainable value.

title: Defence VfI Rubric Generation
section: Worked Example: Defence Digital Support Contract
text:
Worked Example: Defence Digital Support Contract
This example shows how source prompts become a tailored rubric. It is illustrative only.

Context and Value Proposition
A defence organisation is replacing a fragmented set of digital support arrangements with a new managed service for secure applications. The intended value is more reliable support, faster incident resolution, better cyber assurance, lower duplication, clearer supplier accountability, and improved user experience. The main alternatives are retaining current contracts, insourcing more support, using a framework call-off, or creating a hybrid model with retained internal product ownership.
The decision needs to judge whether the proposed managed service represents good resource use before contract award and whether the rubric can later support in-flight benefits evaluation.


Source-to-Rubric Choices







Source prompt
Rubric implication




Verian and OPM require criteria, standards, and mixed evidence before judgement.
Build a rubric rather than relying only on bid scores or savings claims.


Green Book requires objectives, alternatives, unmonetised effects, distribution, and risk.
Include alternatives, user experience, cyber risk, and service continuity alongside cost.


JSP 507 supports whole-life cost, affordability, risk, and evaluation planning.
Require through-life cost, transition cost, retained-client cost, and outturn evaluation evidence.


Sourcing Playbook requires delivery model, should-cost, KPIs, risk allocation, and supplier resilience.
Include commercial discipline, KPI quality, supplier resilience, and risk allocation subcriteria.


GEF requires valid baselines, net savings, recurrence, and no adverse outcome impact.
Treat savings as unproven until baselines, costs to deliver, service quality, and recurrence are evidenced.





Tailored Rubric Extract









Criterion
Programme-specific definition
Subcriteria
Evidence




Economy
The service is economical if the chosen inputs, supplier model, retained-client capability, and transition resources are suitable quality at reasonable whole-life cost.
Should-cost realism; retained-client cost; transition cost; price-quality balance; framework route justification.
Should-cost model, bid evaluation report, delivery model assessment, commercial strategy, contract pricing schedule.


Efficiency
The service is efficient if it reduces duplication and improves support flow without degrading quality, cyber assurance, or user outcomes.
Incident handling productivity; automation and reuse; valid savings claim; reduced handoffs; governance speed.
Service baseline, counterfactual, KPI history, benefits register, GEF-style savings evidence, process maps.


Effectiveness
The service is effective if it improves secure application support in the conditions that matter to defence users.
Incident resolution; user satisfaction; cyber compliance; service availability; support for priority missions.
SLA data, acceptance tests, cyber assurance, user research, operational stakeholder interviews.


Risk and resilience
The service is resilient if supplier, transition, cyber, continuity, and exit risks are owned and managed at an efficient price.
Supplier financial standing; transition risk; exit plan; cyber risk ownership; continuity arrangements.
Supplier assessment, risk allocation matrix, transition plan, continuity plan, contract clauses.


Cost-effectiveness
The service is cost-effective if whole-life cost is justified by better support, reduced duplication, cyber assurance, and credible alternatives analysis.
Options comparison; net benefits; uncertainty; non-monetised value; affordability.
Business case, options appraisal, sensitivity analysis, benefits evidence, unmonetised value narrative.





Example Standards Extract










Criterion
Poor
Adequate
Good
Excellent




Efficiency
Savings depend on reduced service quality, unfunded transition work, weak baselines, or deferred costs.
A plausible savings case exists, but baseline, counterfactual, recurrence, or service-quality protection is incomplete.
Savings and productivity improvements are evidenced as net of transition cost, outcome-protecting, and linked to service improvements.
Realised efficiencies are sustained, independently reviewable, and accompanied by better service quality, cyber assurance, and reusable delivery learning.


Risk and resilience
Key supplier, cyber, transition, or exit risks are unclear, transferred unrealistically, or priced inefficiently.
Main risks are identified, but ownership, mitigations, financial standing, or continuity evidence is partial.
Risks are owned by the party best able to manage them, priced transparently, and supported by credible transition and continuity plans.
Risk management is tested, assured, and adaptive, with clear exit routes, strong supplier resilience, and no hidden transfer of risk to users or future budgets.





Evidence Gaps to Report
If baseline service quality is weak, the evaluator should report that savings confidence is limited. If user evidence is missing, the effectiveness standard should not be rated above adequate without a clear justification. If supplier resilience or exit evidence is commercially sensitive, the report should explain what assurance was reviewed and what cannot be disclosed.
